title: EXPOSÉ VI B GENERALITIES ON GROUP SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VI B GENERALITIES ON GROUP SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VIB
GENERALITIES ON GROUP SCHEMESby J.-E. Bertin
This exposé, which does not correspond to any oral exposé of the seminar, is intended to collect a number of commonly used technical results concerning group schemes.(*)
(*) This exposé has been rather seriously reworked since its mimeographed edition; in particular, §§ 10 and 11 have been entirely rewritten.

pages.title: 1. Morphisms of groups locally of finite type over a field

blocks.html: 1. Morphisms of groups locally of finite type over a field

Let A be an artinian local ring, G and H two A-groups and \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of A-groups. Then u induces a homomorphism of groups \(u(A) : G(A) \to H(A)\). As H(A) acts on H by right translation, \(u(A)\) defines by restriction an action of G(A) on H. This action is compatible with the morphism u and the action of G(A) on G defined by right translation. As G(A) acts transitively on the strictly rational points of G (VIA 0.4), we see that these points "all behave in the same way with respect to u"; from this spring the following properties:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a quasi-compact morphism of A-groups locally of finite type over A. Then the set \(u(G)\) is closed in H and we have \(\dim G = \dim u(G) + \dim \mathrm{Ker}\,u\).

As u commutes with the inverse maps of G and H, the image \(u(G)\) is invariant under the inverse map of H; the same therefore holds for the closure \(\overline{u(G)}\) of \(u(G)\) in H. On the other hand, let L be the set of points of \(H \times_A H\) whose two projections belong to \(u(G)\); it is clear that L is the image of the morphism \(u\times_A u : G\times_A G \to H\times_A H\); hence the multiplication morphism of H sends L into \(u(G)\), in other words \(u(G)\cdot u(G) = u(G)\). On the other hand, lemma 1.2.1 below shows that \(\overline{L}\), the closure of L in H, is the set of points of \(H\times_A H\) whose two projections belong to \(\overline{u(G)}\); hence \(\overline{u(G)}\cdot\overline{u(G)} = \overline{u(G)}\), so that the reduced subscheme of G whose underlying space is \(\overline{u(G)}\) is naturally equipped with a group structure in the category \((\mathbf{Sch}_{/k})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), where k is the residue field of A (cf. VIA 2).

Let us prove the first assertion of 1.2: replacing A if necessary by the algebraic closure of its residue field k, we may assume that A is an algebraically closed field k (cf. EGA IV2, 2.3.12). Replacing u if necessary by \(u_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} : G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \to H_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), we may assume G and H reduced; in this case, as we have just seen, \(u(G)\) is the space underlying a reduced group subscheme of G; we may therefore assume u dominant. Then G(k) acts transitively on the set of connected components of H and it suffices to show that \(u(G) \cap H^0\) is closed: we are reduced to the case where H is connected, hence irreducible and of finite type (VIA 4). Then u is of finite type since it is quasi-compact and locally of finite type; as H is noetherian, \(u(G)\) is constructible (EGA IV1, 1.8.5), hence contains an open set V of H (EGA 0IV, 9.2.3), and then \(H = V\cdot V \subset u(G)\cdot u(G) = u(G)\) (VIA 0.5).

Let us prove the second assertion. First recall that the functor \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) (cf. Exp. I) is representable by \(u^{-1}(e)\), where e denotes the identity element of H. When u is locally of finite type, \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is therefore locally of finite type over A. We reduce, as before, this time thanks to EGA IV2, 4.1.4, to the case where A is an algebraically closed field k, where G and H are irreducible and of finite type, and where u is dominant: indeed, k being algebraically closed, it is clear that the connected components of G, on the set of which G(k) acts transitively, all have the same dimension, and that if \(u^0\) is the restriction of u to \(G^0\), then \((\mathrm{Ker}\,u)^0 \subset \mathrm{Ker}\,u^0\), and \(\dim \mathrm{Ker}\,u^0 = \dim \mathrm{Ker}\,u\). We see likewise that u is then of finite type over k. If h denotes the generic point of H, \(\dim u^{-1}(h) = \dim G - \dim H\) (EGA IV3, 10.6.1 (ii)). By EGA IV3, 9.2.3 and 9.2.6, the set of \(y\in H\) such that \(\dim u^{-1}(y) = \dim u^{-1}(h)\) contains a nonempty open set V. Since u is dominant, \(U = u^{-1}(V)\) is then a nonempty open subset of G, and contains a closed point x of G, since G is a Jacobson scheme (EGA IV3, 10.4.6). Then the right translation \(r_x\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) onto \(u^{-1}(u(x))\), so that: \[ \dim \mathrm{Ker}\,u = \dim u^{-1}(u(x)) = \dim u^{-1}(h) = \dim G - \dim H. \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.1

Let \(f : X' \to X\) and \(g : Y' \to Y\) be two quasi-compact dominant morphisms of schemes over an artinian local ring A; then \(f\times_A g : X'\times_A Y' \to X\times_A Y\) is dominant.

Indeed, we have \(f\times_A g = (f\times_A \mathrm{id}_{Y'})\circ(\mathrm{id}_X\times_A g)\). It therefore suffices to show that \(f\times_A \mathrm{id}_{Y'}\) and \(\mathrm{id}_X\times_A g\) are dominant. For this we may replace A by its residue field k. In this case, X and Y' are flat over \(A=k\), and as \(f\times_A \mathrm{id}_{Y'}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{id}_X\times_A g\)) is deduced from f (resp. g) by the flat base change \(Y'\to A\) (resp. \(X\to A\)), it is dominant (and quasi-compact), by EGA IV2, 2.3.7.

blocks.title: Counterexample
blocks.html:
Counterexample 1.2.2

Let k be a field of characteristic 0, G the constant k-group \(\mathbb{Z}\) and H the additive k-group \(\mathbb{G}_{a,k}\). Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of k-groups. If \(u\neq 0\), then \(u(G)\) is not closed in H.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3

Let k be a field, G a k-group locally of finite type, \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of k-groups, x a point of G. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is quasi-finite (resp. unramified, resp. flat, resp. smooth, resp. étale) at the point x.

  2. (ii)

    u is locally quasi-finite (resp. unramified, resp. flat, resp. smooth, resp. étale).

It suffices to show that (i) implies (ii). By (EGA IV 2.7.1 and 17.7.1), we may assume k algebraically closed and \(x\in G(k)\). Let then U be the nonempty open subset of G consisting of the points where u is quasi-finite (resp. unramified, resp. flat, resp. smooth, resp. étale). It suffices to show that every closed point y of G belongs to U, since G is a Jacobson scheme (EGA IV 10.4.6). There then exists a translation sending x to y, which shows that u is quasi-finite (resp. unramified, resp. flat, resp. smooth, resp. étale) at y.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.1

Let k be a field and G a k-group. Then, for G to be locally quasi-finite (resp. unramified, smooth, étale) over k, it is necessary and sufficient that G be quasi-finite (resp. unramified, smooth, étale) over k at one point.
It suffices to apply 1.3 to the case where H is the unit k-group, taking into account that the set of points at which G is of finite type over k is open, and the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.1.1

Let k be a field and G a k-group. If there exists a nonempty open subset U of G such that U is locally of finite type over k, then G is locally of finite type over k.

Let \(x\in G\) and \(y\in U\). Let \(k'\) be an extension of k that is a compositum of the extensions \(\kappa(x)\) and \(\kappa(y)\). Set \(G' = G\otimes_k k'\), \(U' = U\otimes_k k'\). There then exists a rational point \(x'\in G'\) above x and a rational point \(y'\in U'\) above y. Then \(V' = x'\cdot y'^{-1}\cdot U'\) is an open set containing \(x'\) and locally of finite type over \(k'\). The projection \(G'\to G\) being open (EGA IV2, 2.4.6), the projection of V' onto G is an open subset V of G containing x and locally of finite type over k, by (EGA IV 17.7.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.2

Let A be an artinian local ring, G and H two A-groups locally of finite type, \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of A-groups. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is universally open,

  2. (ii)

    u is open,

  3. (iii)

    u is open at a point of G,

  4. (iv)

    the map \(u^0 : G^0 \to H^0\) deduced from u is dominant, or what amounts to the same thing (1.2 and VIA 4), \(u^0\) is surjective,

  5. (v)

    there exists a connected component \(\widehat{G}\) of G such that, if \(\widehat{H}\) denotes the connected component of H containing \(u(\widehat{G})\), the map \(\widehat{u} : \widehat{G} \to \widehat{H}\) deduced from u is dominant.

It is clear that (i) implies (ii), and that (ii) implies (iii). Since \(G^0\) (resp. \(\widehat{G}\)) is open in G (VIA 3) and \(H^0\) (resp. \(\widehat{H}\)) is irreducible (VIA 4.1), we see that (ii) implies (iv) (resp. that (iii) implies (v)). It thus remains to show that (v) implies (i) (for, (iv) being a special case of (v), this will also show that (iv) implies (i)). The open set \(\widehat{G}\) (resp. \(\widehat{H}\)) of G (resp. H) will be equipped with its induced scheme structure, and \(\widehat{u}\) will denote the morphism \(\widehat{u} : \widehat{G} \to \widehat{H}\) deduced from u. Let k be the residue field of A. Since \(\widehat{G}\) is quasi-compact over A (VIA 4.1) and \(\widehat{H}\) is separated over A (VIA 2), \(\widehat{u}\) is quasi-compact, hence \(\widehat{u}\otimes_A k\) is quasi-compact and dominant; the same holds for \(\widehat{u}\otimes_A \overline{k}\) (where \(\overline{k}\) denotes the algebraic closure of k) by (EGA IV 2.3.7). Since \(\widehat{G}\otimes_A \overline{k}\) is a union of connected components of \(G\otimes_A \overline{k}\), it is clear that \(G\otimes_A \overline{k}\), \(H\otimes_A \overline{k}\), and \(u\otimes_A \overline{k}\) satisfy assertion (v). We are thus reduced to the case where \(A = k\) is an algebraically closed field, taking into account (EGA IV 2.6.4). In this case, we may moreover replace u by \(u_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), and we are reduced to the case where H is reduced. Then \(\widehat{H}\) is reduced, and the generic flatness theorem (EGA IV2, 6.9.1) asserts that, since \(\widehat{u}\) is dominant, \(\widehat{u}\) (hence also u) is flat at the generic point of \(\widehat{G}\), so that u is flat (1.3), hence universally open (EGA IV2, 2.4.6).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4

Let A be an artinian local ring, G and H two A-groups locally of finite type, and \(u : G \to H\) a quasi-compact morphism of A-groups. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is proper,

  2. (ii)

    there exists \(h\in H\) such that the fiber \(u^{-1}(h)\) is nonempty and proper over \(\kappa(h)\),

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is proper.

It is clear that (i) implies (iii), and that (iii) implies (ii). On the other hand, it follows from the hypotheses that u is of finite type and separated (EGA I 5.5.1) (since G is separated (VIA 2)). It therefore remains to show that assertion (ii) implies that u is universally closed, so that we may assume A equal to its residue field k, and even that k is algebraically closed and that \(h\in H(k)\) (EGA IV 2.6.4). We have seen (1.2) that then \(u(G)\) is the set underlying a closed reduced group subscheme of H; every closed immersion being proper (EGA II, 5.4.2), we may assume that u is surjective, and that H is reduced. Since u is surjective, the group G(k) acts transitively on the set of closed points of H; for every closed point y of H, \(u^{-1}(y)\) is therefore proper over \(\kappa(y)\). By (EGA IV 9.6.1), the set of \(y\in H\) such that \(u^{-1}(y)\) is not proper over \(k(y)\) is locally constructible; since it contains no closed point, it is empty (EGA IV 10.1.2). Consider then the generic point of \(H^0\). By (EGA IV 8.10.5) used according to the method of 8.1.2 b), there exists a nonempty open subset V of \(H^0\) such that the restriction of u above the open set V is proper. It is then clear that the \(g\cdot V\) (\(g\in G(k)\)) form an open covering of H such that, for every \(g\in G(k)\), the restriction of u above the open set \(g\cdot V\) is proper; we deduce that u is proper (EGA IV 2.4.3 (vii)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.1

Let A be an artinian local ring, G and H two A-groups locally of finite type, \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of A-groups. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is locally quasi-finite,

  2. (ii)

    u is quasi-finite at a point,

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is discrete,

  4. (iv)

    the restriction of u to each connected component of G is finite.

Finally, if u is quasi-compact, these assertions are equivalent to the following one:

  1. (v)

    u is finite.

It is clear that (iv) implies (iii), that (iii) implies (ii) (EGA I, 6.4.4), and that in the case where u is quasi-compact, assertions (iv) and (v) are equivalent. We have already seen in 1.3 that assertions (i) and (ii) are equivalent. Let us finally show that (i) implies (iv). Let \(\widehat{G}\) be a connected component of G; since \(\widehat{G}\) is of finite type over A (VIA 4.1) and H is separated (VIA 2), the restriction \(\widehat{u}\) of u to \(\widehat{G}\) is of finite type and separated, since \(\widehat{G}\) is separated over A (EGA I 5.5.1). It therefore suffices to show that \(\widehat{u}\) is universally closed; we may thus assume that A is equal to its residue field k, and even that k is algebraically closed (EGA IV 2.6.4), for \(\widehat{G}\otimes_A \overline{k}\), where \(\overline{k}\) denotes the algebraic closure of k, is the sum of a finite number of connected components of \(G\otimes_A \overline{k}\). Let then g be a closed point of \(\widehat{G}\); if \(u^0 : G^0 \to H\) is the restriction of u to \(G^0\), we have \(\widehat{u} = r_{u(g^{-1})}\circ u^0\circ r_g\), where \(r_g\) denotes right translation by g, and to show that \(\widehat{u}\) is proper, it suffices to show that \(u^0\) is proper. By hypothesis, u is locally quasi-finite, hence the fiber \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is discrete (and nonempty); we have seen that \(u^0\) is of finite type, hence the fiber \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u^0\) is finite (EGA II 6.2.2), hence proper, and nonempty; hence \(u^0\) is proper (1.4), hence finite (EGA III 4.4.2) since it is quasi-finite.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.2

Let A be an artinian local ring, G and H two A-groups locally of finite type, \(u : G \to H\) a quasi-compact morphism of A-groups. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is a closed immersion,

  2. (ii)

    u is a monomorphism,

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is isomorphic to the unit k-group.

In particular, every group subscheme of H is closed.

It is clear that (i) implies (ii), and if we consider the functors represented respectively by G and H, it is immediate that conditions (ii) and (iii) are equivalent. Finally, if \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is the unit k-group, \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is a proper nonempty fiber, hence (1.4), u is a proper monomorphism, and of finite presentation since H is locally noetherian (EGA IV 6.1), hence is a closed immersion (EGA IV3, 8.11.5). The last assertion follows from the fact that, since H is locally noetherian, every immersion \(G\to H\) is quasi-compact (EGA I, 6.6.4).

blocks.title: Counterexample
blocks.html:
Counterexample 1.4.3

Let k be a field of characteristic 0, G the constant k-group \(\mathbb{Z}\) and H the k-group \(\mathbb{G}_a\). Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of k-groups. If \(u\neq 0\), then \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u = 0\), but u is not a closed immersion (cf. 1.2.2).
We shall use later the following two results, which should have appeared in Exposé VIA:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5

Let k be a field and G a k-group locally of finite type. Every irreducible component of G has the same dimension as G; in other words, for every \(g\in G\), we have \(\dim_g G = \dim G\).

The two assertions are equivalent (EGA IV3, 10.6.3 and 10.7.1). Let \(\widehat{G}\) be an irreducible component of G. Then, if \(\overline{k}\) denotes the algebraic closure of k, \(\widehat{G}\otimes_k \overline{k}\) is a union of irreducible components of \(G\otimes_k \overline{k}\) (EGA IV 4.4.1); we are thus reduced to the case where k is algebraically closed (EGA IV 4.1.4). In this case, \(\widehat{G}\) is deduced from \(G^0\) by translation, hence \(\dim \widehat{G} = \dim G^0 = \dim G\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6

Let S be a scheme of characteristic zero, G an S-group scheme locally of finite presentation along the unit section e. For G to be smooth over S along the unit section, it is necessary and sufficient that the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\omega_{G/S} = \varepsilon^*(\Omega^1_{G/S})\) (called the Module conormal to the unit section of G) be locally free.
Recall that a scheme S is said to be of characteristic zero if for every closed point x of S, the field \(\kappa(x)\) is of characteristic zero.

Recall also (II 11) that, if \(\pi\) denotes the structural morphism \(G\to S\), we have \(\Omega^1_{G/S} = \pi^*(\omega_{G/S})\), so that it amounts to the same thing to say that the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\omega_{G/S}\) is locally free, or that the \(\mathscr{O}_G\)-Module \(\Omega^1_{G/S}\) is locally free.
The proposition is then an immediate consequence of (EGA IV4, 16.12.2 and 17.12.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.6.1 (Cartier)

Given a field k of characteristic zero, every k-group locally of finite type over k is smooth over k.
Indeed, it is then clear that the k-Module \(\omega_{G/k}\) is locally free, hence (1.7) G is smooth over k at the unit point e, and hence smooth over k (1.3.1).

pages.title: 2. "Open properties" of groups and of group morphisms locally of finite presentation

blocks.html: 2. "Open properties" of groups and of group morphisms locally of finite presentation

In all that follows, S will denote an arbitrary scheme; an S-group scheme will be called an S-group G. Given an S-group G, we shall denote by \(\pi\) or \(\pi_G\) the structural morphism \(G\to S\), by \(\varepsilon\) the unit section, by c the inverse map and by \(\mu\) the multiplication morphism \(G\times_S G \to G\). Given a property \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) for a morphism of S-schemes \(u : X \to Y\), we shall say that \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is stable under base change if, whenever u satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\), the same holds for the morphism \(u_{(Y')}\), for every S-morphism \(Y'\to Y\).
We say that \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is of a local nature for the topology T (cf. IV 4 and 6) if \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) satisfies the two following conditions:
a) \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is stable under base change,

b) whenever there exists a family of S-morphisms \(Y_i \to Y\) that is covering for the topology T and such that each of the morphisms \(u_{(Y_i)}\) satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\), then u satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) be a property for a morphism of S-schemes. Suppose that \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is of a local nature for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology. Let G and H be two S-groups and \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of S-groups. Suppose G flat and universally open over S. Let W be the largest open subset of H above which u satisfies the property \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) and let \(V = u^{-1}(W)\). Then there exists an open subset U of S such that V is an open group subscheme of \(G\,|\,U\).

The existence of a largest open subset W of H above which u satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) follows from the fact that \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is of a local nature for the Zariski topology. Since \(\pi_G\) is universally open, \(\pi_G(V)\) is an open subset U of S. It suffices to show that V is a group subscheme of \(G|U\). We may therefore assume that \(U = S\).

Then set \(G' = G\times_S V\), \(H' = H\times_S V\), \(V' = V\times_S V\), \(W' = W\times_S V\) and \(u' = u_{(V)}\); let \(W'_1\) be the largest open subset of H' above which \(u'\) satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\); since V is flat and universally open over S, the same holds for H' over H, and lemma 2.1.1 below shows that \(W'_1 = W'\). Consider then the automorphism of V-schemes a (resp. b) of G' (resp. H'), right translation by the inverse of the diagonal section \(\delta\) (resp. by the inverse of \(u(\delta)\)), defined by \(a(g,v) = (g\cdot v^{-1}, v)\), (resp. \(b(h,v) = (h\cdot u(v^{-1}), v)\)), for every morphism \(T\to S\), \(g\in G(T)\), \(v\in V(T)\) and \(h\in H(T)\). It is clear that \(u'\circ a = b\circ u'\), which shows that W' is stable under b, hence that V' is stable under a, so that V is a group subscheme of G.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.1

Let \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) be a property for an S-morphism u. Suppose \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) of a local nature for the fpqc topology. Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an S-morphism of schemes, and let \(g : Y' \to Y\) be a flat and open S-morphism. Let W (resp. \(W'_1\)) be the largest open subset of Y (resp. Y') above which f (resp. \(f' = f_{(Y')}\)) satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\). Then \(W'_1 = W\times_Y Y'\).

Set \(W' = W\times_Y Y'\); since \(\mathscr{P}(u)\) is stable under base change, it is clear that \(W'\subset W'_1\). Set \(W_1 = g(W'_1)\), \(V_1 = f^{-1}(W_1)\) and \(V'_1 = V_1\times_{W_1} W'_1\); it is clear that \(V'_1 = f'^{-1}(W'_1)\). Since g is flat and open, the morphism \(W'_1\to W_1\) deduced from g is faithfully flat and open, hence covering for the (fpqc) topology. Since the morphism \(V'_1\to W'_1\) deduced from \(f'\) satisfies \(\mathscr{P}(u)\), the same holds for the morphism \(V_1\to W_1\) deduced from f, hence \(W_1\subset W\), and \(W'_1\subset g^{-1}(W_1)\subset g^{-1}(W) = W'\); hence \(W' = W'_1\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.1.2

A great number of properties for a morphism are of a local nature for the (fpqc) topology (cf. EGA IV2, 2.6 and 2.7); let us mention those of being flat, smooth, unramified, étale (EGA IV4, 17.7.3), locally quasi-finite.

The proof of 2.1 in fact uses only base changes by flat morphisms; the proposition will therefore apply to a property satisfying condition b) of 2.0 relative to the (fpqc) topology, and stable under base changes by flat morphisms (example: that of being quasi-compact and dominant).

Of course, one can state an analogous proposition concerning properties of a local nature for a topology T finer than the Zariski topology, the condition to be verified on G being then that \(\pi_G\) be universally open and covering for the topology T.

In particular, if G is flat and locally of finite presentation over S, there is an analogous statement for properties stable under base changes by flat morphisms locally of finite presentation, and satisfying condition b) of 2.0 relative to the (fpqc) topology (for example, those of being regular, reduced, Cohen-Macaulay, etc... (EGA IV2, 6.8)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2

Let G and H be two S-groups and \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of S-groups. Then:

  1. (i)

    Suppose G or H flat over S, and G or H locally of finite presentation over S; then the largest open subset V of G such that the restriction of u to V is flat and locally of finite presentation (resp. smooth, resp. étale) is an open group subscheme of \(G|U\), where U denotes a suitable open subset of S.

  2. (ii)

    Suppose G or H universally open over S; then the largest open subset V of G such that the restriction of u to V is universally open is an open group subscheme of \(G|U\), where U is a suitable open subset of S.

Let us first prove (i). Let us show that the restriction \(\pi_V\) of \(\pi_G\) to V is flat and locally of finite presentation: a) If \(\pi_G\) is flat (resp. locally of finite presentation), the same holds for \(\pi_V\). b) If \(\pi_H\) is flat (resp. locally of finite presentation), the same holds for \(\pi_V\), as the composite of the restriction of u to V and \(\pi_H\).

Hence, in the four cases envisaged in the statement, \(\pi_V\) is flat and locally of finite presentation, hence universally open (EGA IV2, 2.4.6). Set \(U = \pi_V(V)\); U is therefore an open subset of S. It then suffices to show that V is an open group subscheme of \(G|U\); we may therefore assume that \(U = S\).

Then set \(G' = G\times_S V\), \(H' = H\times_S V\), \(V' = V\times_S V\) and \(u' = u_{(V)}\). Then, V being flat and locally of finite presentation over S, the same holds for H' over H. By EGA IV4, 17.7.4, V' is then the largest open subset of G' such that the restriction of \(u'\) to V' is flat and locally of finite presentation (resp. smooth, resp. étale). The automorphisms a and b being defined as in the proof of 2.1, it is then clear that V' is stable under a, hence that V is a group subscheme of G.

Let us prove (ii). The restriction \(\pi_V\) of \(\pi_G\) to V is a universally open morphism, either because the same holds for \(\pi_G\), or as the composite of the restriction of u to V and \(\pi_H\) in the case where \(\pi_H\) is universally open. Set \(U = \pi_V(V)\); U is then an open subset of S. It suffices to show that V is an open group subscheme of \(G|U\). We may therefore assume that \(U = S\).

Set as before \(G' = G\times_S V\), \(H' = H\times_S V\), \(V' = V\times_S V\) and \(u' = u_{(V)}\). Then \(\pi_V : V \to S\) is surjective and universally open, and the same holds for \(G'\to G\), so that V' is the largest open subset of G' such that the restriction of \(u'\) to V' is universally open, by virtue of (EGA IV3, 14.3.4 and (ii)). The automorphisms a and b being defined as before, it is then clear that V' is stable under a, hence that V is a group subscheme of G.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let G be an S-group. The largest open subset V of G such that V is flat and locally of finite presentation (resp. smooth, étale, universally open) over S is an open group subscheme of \(G|U\), where U denotes a suitable open subset of S.
It suffices to apply 2.2 to the case where H is the unit S-group and u is the unique morphism of S-groups \(G\to H\), for then \(\pi_H\) is a morphism and \(\pi_G = \pi_H\circ u\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let G be an S-group; suppose that there exists a neighborhood X of the unit section such that X is flat and locally of finite presentation (resp. smooth, resp. étale, resp. universally open) over S; then there exists an open group subscheme V of G such that V is flat and locally of finite presentation (resp. smooth, resp. étale, resp. universally open) over S.
It suffices to apply 2.3, remarking that here, with the notation of 2.2, we have \(\varepsilon(S)\subset V\), hence \(U = S\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of S-groups.

  1. (i)

    Suppose that G is flat and of finite presentation over S at the points of its unit section, and that H is of finite presentation over S at the points of its unit section. Then the set U of points \(s\in S\) such that \(u_s\) is flat (resp. smooth, resp. étale) is open in S.

    If, in particular, we suppose G flat over S, and G and H locally of finite presentation over S, then the set V of the points of G where u is flat (resp. smooth, resp. étale) is equal to \(G|U\).

  2. (ii)

    Suppose that u is locally of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite type) at the points of the unit section of G, and that, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is locally of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\) (a condition satisfied if G is of finite type over S at the points of the unit section (1.3.1.1)). Then the set U of the \(s\in S\) such that \(u_s\) is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite) is open in S.

    If we suppose moreover that u is locally of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite type), then the set V of the points of G where u is unramified (resp. quasi-finite) is equal to \(G|U\).

Let us prove (i). First note (1.3.1.1) that, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is locally of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\). Let Y be the set of the points of H where \(\pi_H\) is of finite presentation, and let X be the set of the points of \(u^{-1}(Y)\) where \(\pi_G\) is flat and of finite presentation. Recall (EGA IV 1.4.2 and 11.3.1) that X (resp. Y) is open in G (resp. H). Let \(u_X : X \to Y\) be the morphism deduced from u. Since Y (resp. X) contains the unit section of H (resp. G), the restriction \(\pi_X\) of \(\pi_G\) to X is surjective, and the morphism \(S \to X\) deduced from \(\varepsilon_G\) is an S-section of X. Let \(V_X\) be the set of the points of X where \(u_X\) is flat (resp. smooth, resp. étale). Let \(x\in X\) and set \(s = \pi_X(x)\); then by (EGA IV 11.3.10), (resp. 11.3.10 and 17.5.1, resp. 11.3.10 and 17.6.1), x belongs to \(V_X\) if and only if \(u_s\) is flat (resp. smooth, resp. étale) at the point x, or, what amounts to the same thing (1.3), if and only if \(u_s\) is flat (resp. smooth, resp. étale). Consequently, we have \(U = \varepsilon_G^{-1}(V_X)\) and \(V_X = \pi_X^{-1}(U)\). Recall (EGA IV 11.3.1 (resp. 17.3.7)) that \(V_X\) is open in X, hence in G, so that U is open in S.
The second assertion follows from what we have just seen, for then \(X = G\), \(Y = H\), \(V_X = V\), hence \(V = \pi_G^{-1}(U)\).

Assertion (ii) is proved in an analogous manner, using (1.3) and the fact that, for an S-morphism \(u : X \to Y\) locally of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite type) to be unramified (resp. quasi-finite) at a point \(x\in X\), it is necessary and sufficient that, if s denotes the projection of x onto S, \(u_s\) be unramified (resp. quasi-finite) at the point x (EGA IV 17.4.2 and ErrIII 20).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of S-groups that is a radicial morphism (which is the case if u is a monomorphism (EGA I, 3.5.4)). Suppose G and H locally of finite presentation over S and G flat over S. Then the set U of the \(s\in S\) such that \(u_s\) is an open immersion is open in S, and the restriction of u above U is an open immersion.

By 2.5 (i), the set U' of the points \(s\in S\) such that \(u_s\) is étale is open in S. Since u is radicial, so is \(u_s\), for every \(s\in S\), hence by EGA IV4, 17.9.1, we have \(U = U'\), which shows that U is open. Finally, by 2.5 (i), the restriction of u above U is étale; since u is radicial, this restriction is an open immersion (EGA IV4, 17.9.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7

Let G be an S-group. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    G is unramified over S at the points of the unit section,

  2. (ii)

    the unit section is an open immersion,

  3. (iii)

    G is of finite presentation over S at the points of the unit section, and for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is unramified over \(\kappa(s)\).

If, moreover, we suppose G locally of finite presentation over S, then each of the three preceding conditions is equivalent to the following one:

  1. (iv)

    G is unramified over S.

The equivalence of assertions (i) and (ii) follows from the more general lemma 2.7.1 below. Note (1.3.1.1) that either of conditions (i) or (iii) implies that, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is locally of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\). Then (EGA IV4, 17.4.1), assertion (i) is equivalent to the fact that, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is unramified over \(\kappa(s)\) at the point \(e_s\), the unit of \(G_s\), or again (1.3.1), to the fact that \(G_s\) is unramified over \(\kappa(s)\), hence assertions (i) and (iii) are equivalent. Finally, if G is locally of finite presentation over S, assertions (iii) and (iv) are equivalent (EGA IV4, 17.4.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.7.1

Let G be an S-scheme equipped with a section \(\varepsilon\). For G to be unramified over S at the points of this section, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\varepsilon\) be an open immersion.

We already know that the condition is necessary (EGA IV4, 17.4.1 a) ⇒ b'')). Conversely, if \(\varepsilon\) is an open immersion, then the restriction to \(\varepsilon(S)\) of the structural morphism \(C \to S\) is an isomorphism, hence G is unramified over S at the points of \(\varepsilon(S)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a morphism of S-groups. Suppose that u is locally of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite type) and that for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is locally of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    u is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite),

  2. (ii)

    for every \(s\in S\), \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite),

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite) over S,

  4. (iv)

    the unit section \(S \to \mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is an open immersion (resp. the fibers of \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) are discrete).

It is clear that assertions (i) and (ii) are equivalent (EGA IV4, 17.4.1 resp. ErrIII 20). Reminder 2.8.1 below shows that (i) implies (iii). Moreover, (iii) implies that, for every \(s\in S\), if \(e_s\) denotes the unit element of \(H_s\), \((\mathrm{Ker}\,u)_s = \mathrm{Ker}\,u_s = u_s^{-1}(e_s)\) is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite) over \(k(s)\), hence (since \(k(s)\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } k(e_s)\)) that \(u_s\) is unramified (resp. quasi-finite) at the unit point of \(G_s\), hence that \(u_s\) is unramified (resp. locally quasi-finite) (1.3). Hence (iii) implies (ii). Finally, assertions (iii) and (iv) are equivalent (2.7).

blocks.title: Reminder
blocks.html:
Reminder 2.8.1

Recall (I) that, given a morphism \(u : G \to H\) of S-groups, we call the kernel of u, denoted \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\), the group subfunctor of G defined by setting, for every morphism \(f : T \to S\), \[ \mathrm{Ker}\,u(T) = \{a\in G(T) \mid u\circ a = \varepsilon_H\circ f\}. \] It is clear (EGA I 4.4.1) that this functor is representable by the S-group \(G\times_H S = u^{-1}(\varepsilon_H(S))\), denoted simply \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\). In particular, the structural morphism \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u \to S\) is deduced from u by base change.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9

Let \(\pi : G \to S\) be a morphism admitting an S-section \(\varepsilon\).

  1. (i)

    If \(\pi\) is injective, it is integral.(*)

  2. (ii)

    If \(\pi\) is locally of finite type, and if, for every \(s\in S\), \(\pi_s\) is an isomorphism, then \(\pi\) is an isomorphism.(**)

(*) This is also a special case of EGA IV4, 18.12.11, for \(\pi\) is evidently a universal homeomorphism.
(**) This is also an immediate consequence of EGA IV4, 18.12.6.

First note that, by lemma 2.9.1 below, \(\pi\), being a homeomorphism, is an affine morphism. If \(\pi\) is injective, \(\varepsilon\) is surjective. Since \(\varepsilon\) is a surjective immersion, \(\varepsilon(S)\) is isomorphic to the closed subscheme of G defined by a Nilideal \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\). Since \(\varepsilon\) is an S-section of the morphism \(\pi\), we have a direct sum decomposition \(\mathscr{O}_X = \mathscr{O}_S\oplus\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules. Since \(\mathscr{I}\) is a Nilideal of \(\mathscr{O}_X\), \(\mathscr{I}\) is evidently integral over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), hence \(\mathscr{O}_X\) is integral over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), and \(\pi\) is integral.

Now suppose that \(\pi\) is locally of finite type. Then \(\varepsilon\) is locally of finite presentation (EGA IV 1.4.3 (v)), hence \(\mathscr{I}\) is an ideal of finite type of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) (EGA IV1, 1.4.1). For every \(s\in S\), we have \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s} = \kappa(s)\oplus\mathscr{I}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\kappa(s)\). By hypothesis, \(\varepsilon_s\) is an isomorphism, hence \(\mathscr{I}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\kappa(s) = 0\), for every \(s\in S\), hence a fortiori \(\mathscr{I}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_X}\kappa(x) = 0\) for every \(x\in X\), which implies, by Nakayama's lemma, that \(\mathscr{I} = 0\), hence that \(\pi\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9.1

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of schemes that is a homeomorphism; then \(f\) is an affine morphism.(*)
(*) cf. EGA IV4, 18.12.7.1 for a slightly more general result, provable by the same proof.

It suffices to show that every point \(y\in Y\) has an open neighborhood W such that the restriction of f above W is an affine morphism. So let \(y\in Y\), and let V be an affine open neighborhood of y in Y. Let \(V' = f^{-1}(V)\). Then V' is an open neighborhood of \(x = f^{-1}(y)\) in X. There exists an affine open neighborhood W' of x in X contained in V'. Then set \(W = f(W')\). Then W is an open neighborhood of y in Y contained in the affine scheme V, hence W is separated. Since W' is an affine scheme, the restriction of f above W is then an affine morphism (EGA II, 1.2.3).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10

Let G be an S-group locally of finite type. Suppose that, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is the unit \(\kappa(s)\)-group; then G is the unit S-group.
More generally:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.11

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an S-morphism locally of finite type. For f to be a monomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(s\in S\), \(f_s\) be a monomorphism.

It is clear that the condition is necessary; let us show that it is sufficient. If, for every \(s\in S\), \(f_s\) is a monomorphism, then a fortiori for every \(y\in Y\), \(f_y\) is a monomorphism; we may therefore assume that \(Y = S\). By EGA I, 5.3.8, to show that f is a monomorphism, it suffices to show that \(\Delta_f : X \to X\times_S X\) is an isomorphism, or, what amounts to the same thing, that the first projection \(p : X\times_S X \to X\) is an isomorphism. But, if \(f_s\) is a monomorphism, it follows likewise from EGA I, 5.3.8, that the first projection \(X_s\times_{\kappa(s)} X_s \to X_s\) (which is identified with \(p_s\)) is an isomorphism. Now p has the S-section \(\Delta_f\), so lemma 2.9 asserts that if for every \(s\in S\), \(p_s\) is an isomorphism, then the same holds for p, q.e.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.12

Let G be an S-scheme having an S-section \(\varepsilon\). Suppose that the structural morphism \(\pi : G \to S\) is closed. Let \(s\in S\) be such that \(\pi\) is of finite presentation at the point \(\varepsilon(s)\), and that \(\varepsilon_s : \mathrm{Spec}(\kappa(s)) \to G_s\) is an isomorphism (or, what amounts to the same thing, that \(\pi_s : G_s \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s)\) is an isomorphism). Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s in S such that \(\varepsilon\times_S U : U \to G\times_S U\) is an isomorphism.

Let V be the set of the points of G where \(\pi\) is unramified; we know that V is open (EGA 17.3.7) and contains \(\varepsilon(s)\). Hence \(U = \varepsilon^{-1}(V)\) is an open subset of S containing s, and such that for every \(t\in U\), \(\pi\) is unramified at \(\varepsilon(t)\). One easily checks that if \(\pi\) is closed, the same holds for \(\pi\times_S U\), so we may assume that \(U = S\).

Then \(G - \varepsilon(S)\) is a closed subset X of G (2.7.1), not meeting \(G_s\); hence, since \(\pi\) is closed, \(\pi(X)\) is a closed subset F of S not meeting s; set \(U = S - F\); then U is an open subset of S such that \(\varepsilon\times_S U\) is an isomorphism of U onto \(G|U\).

pages.title: 3. Identity component of a group locally of finite presentation

blocks.html: 3. Identity component of a group locally of finite presentation

Given a subset A (resp. B) of an S-scheme X (resp. Y), by abuse of notation, \(A\times_S B\) will denote the subset of \(X\times_S Y\) consisting of the points whose first projection belongs to A and whose second belongs to B.
Given a subset A of an S-group G, we shall say that A is stable under the group law of G if we have: \(c(A)\subset A\) and \(\mu(A\times_S A)\subset A\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let T be an S-group functor satisfying the following condition:

(+) for every \(s\in S\), the functor \(T_s = T\otimes_S \kappa(s)\) is representable.

Let then \(T^0_s\) be the connected component of the identity element of the \(\kappa(s)\)-group \(T_s\). One defines an S-group subfunctor of T, called the identity component of T, denoted \(T^0\), by setting, for every morphism \(S'\to S\): \[ T^0(S') = \{u\in T(S') \mid \forall\, s\in S,\ u_s(S'_s) \subset T^0_s\}. \] We have thus defined the functor \(T\mapsto T^0\) from \((\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S})\text{-gr.}\) to \((\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S})\text{-gr.}\)

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.2

(i) Let T be an S-group functor satisfying condition (+); then \(\mathrm{Lie}(T^0/S) = \mathrm{Lie}(T/S)\), by virtue of exposé II.

(ii) If T and T' are two S-group functors satisfying (+), then:

a) if \(T\subset T'\), then \(T^0\subset T'^0\),

b) if \(T\subset T'\) and \(T'^0\subset T\), then \(T^0 = T'^0\),

c) if for every \(s\in S\), \(T_s\) is locally of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\), then \(T^0\) satisfies property (+), and we have \((T^0)^0 = T^0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

Let T be an S-group functor satisfying condition (+) and let S' be an S-scheme: then \((T\times_S S')^0 = T^0\times_S S'\); in other words the functor \(T\mapsto T^0\) commutes with base change, i.e. the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ (\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S})\text{-gr.} \ar[r]^-{T\mapsto T^0} \ar[d] & (\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S})\text{-gr.} \ar[d] \\ (\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S'})\text{-gr.} \ar[r]_-{T\mapsto T^0} & (\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}}_{/S'})\text{-gr.} } \]

It suffices indeed to check that, for every \(s'\in S'\), whose image in S we denote by s, we have: \(T^0_s\otimes_{\kappa(s)}\kappa(s') = ((T\times_S S')\otimes_{S'}\kappa(s'))^0\), which follows from VIA 1.1, for \((T\times_S S')\otimes_{S'}\kappa(s') = T_s\otimes_{\kappa(s)}\kappa(s')\).

blocks.title: Special case
blocks.html:
Special case 3.4

Let G be an S-group scheme; denote by \(\underline{G^0}\) the subset of G that is the union of the \(G^0_s\) as s runs through S. Then \(\underline{G^0}\) is a subset of G stable under the group law of G (cf. 3.0), and for every morphism \(S'\to S\) we have: \[ G^0(S') = \{u\in G(S') \mid u(S') \subset \underline{G^0}\}. \] When \(\underline{G^0}\) is an open subset of G, it is clear that \(G^0\) is representable by the subscheme of G induced on the open set \(\underline{G^0}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

Let S be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, and G an S-group with fibers locally of finite type. Then there exists a quasi-compact open subset U of G containing \(\underline{G^0}\).

The unit section \(\varepsilon\) being an immersion, \(\varepsilon(S)\) is a quasi-compact subspace of G, hence there exists a quasi-compact open subset V of G containing \(\varepsilon(S)\). Since S is quasi-separated, and V is quasi-compact, V is quasi-compact over S (EGA IV1, 1.2.4), hence \(V\times_S V\) is quasi-compact over S, hence quasi-compact. Then \(V\cdot V = (V\times_S V)\) is quasi-compact. Set \(V_s = V\cap G_s\), \(V^0_s = V\cap G^0_s\). Then \(V^0_s\) is an open subset of \(G^0_s\), dense in \(G^0_s\) since \(G^0_s\) is irreducible (VIA 4), hence \(V^0_s\cdot V^0_s = G^0_s\) (VIA 0.5), which shows that \(V_{s.s}\supset G^0_s\), hence \(V\cdot V\supset \underline{G^0}\). Finally, since \(V\cdot V\) is quasi-compact, there exists a quasi-compact open subset U of G containing \(V\cdot V\) and a fortiori \(\underline{G^0}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Let G be an S-group with fibers locally of finite type and connected. Then G is quasi-compact over S.

Our assertion being local on S (EGA I, 6.6.1), we are reduced to the case where S is affine; by 3.5, there then exists a quasi-compact open subset U of G containing \(\underline{G^0} = G\), hence G is quasi-compact, hence quasi-compact over the affine scheme S (EGA I, 6.6.1 (v)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation; then \(\underline{G^0}\) is ind-constructible in G. If we suppose that G is quasi-separated over S, and that S is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, then \(\underline{G^0}\) is constructible. Consequently, if G is quasi-separated over S, \(\underline{G^0}\) is locally constructible.

Let us first prove the first assertion. Since \(\pi\) is locally of finite presentation, given \(s\in S\), there exist an open subset U of G containing \(\varepsilon(s)\) and an open subset V of S containing s such that \(\pi(U)\subset V\) and such that the morphism \(\pi' : U \to V\) deduced from \(\pi\) is of finite presentation. Then set \(T = \varepsilon^{-1}(U)\) and \(W = \pi'^{-1}(T)\). Then the morphism \(\pi'' : W \to T\) deduced from \(\pi'\) is of finite presentation, and admits as a section the morphism \(\varepsilon'' : T \to W\) deduced from \(\varepsilon\). For every \(t\in T\), \(G^0_t\) being irreducible (VIA 4), \(W\cap G^0_t\) is dense in \(G^0_t\), hence irreducible, hence connected: it is therefore the connected component of \(\pi''^{-1}(t)\) containing \(\varepsilon''(t)\). The union \(W^0\) of the \(W\cap G^0_t\), for \(t\in T\), is locally constructible in W, by (EGA IV 9.7.12). But it follows from (VIA 0.5) that \(W^0\cdot W^0\) is equal to \(\underline{G^0}\cap\pi^{-1}(T)\). Now, since \(\pi\) is locally of finite presentation, the same holds for \(\mu\) (VIA 1), hence for the morphism \(\mu'' : W\times_T W \to \pi^{-1}(T)\) deduced from \(\mu\). Consequently \(\underline{G^0}\cap\pi^{-1}(T) = \mu''(W^0\times_T W^0)\) is ind-constructible in \(\pi^{-1}(T)\) (EGA IV1, 1.9.5 (viii)), for, since \(W^0\) is locally constructible in W, it is clear that \(W^0\times_T W^0\) is locally constructible in \(W\times_T W\). This shows that \(\underline{G^0}\) is ind-constructible in G.

Now suppose S quasi-compact and quasi-separated and G quasi-separated over S; then (3.5), there exists a quasi-compact open subset U of G containing \(\underline{G^0}\). Since G is quasi-separated over S, G is quasi-separated, hence the open set U is retrocompact (EGA IV1, 1.2.7), and it suffices to show that \(\underline{G^0}\) is constructible in U (EGA 0III, 9.1.8). Moreover, U being quasi-compact, hence quasi-compact over S (EGA IV1, 1.2.4), and quasi-separated over S, the restriction of \(\pi\) to U is of finite presentation, and by (EGA IV3, 9.7.12), \(\underline{G^0}\) is locally constructible in U, hence constructible in U, since U is quasi-compact and quasi-separated (EGA IV1, 1.8.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(S_0\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, I an increasing filtered preordered set, \((\mathscr{A}_i)_{i\in I}\) an inductive system of commutative quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-Algebras, \(\mathscr{A} = \varinjlim \mathscr{A}_i\), \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}_i\) for \(i\in I\), and \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}\) (cf. EGA II, 1.3.1). Let G be an \(S_0\)-group scheme locally of finite presentation. Then the canonical map \(\varinjlim G^0(S_i) \to G^0(S)\) is bijective.

Since G is locally of finite presentation over S, the canonical map \(\varinjlim G(S_i) \to G(S)\) is bijective, by EGA IV2, 8.14.2 c). It follows immediately that the canonical map \(\varinjlim G^0(S_i)\to G^0(S)\) is injective. Let us show that it is surjective. Let \(s\in G^0(S)\subset G(S)\). There exists \(i\subset I\) such that s factors by means of \(s_i\in G(S_i)\) through \(S\to S_i\); by hypothesis, \(s^{-1}(\underline{G^0}) = S\). But, by 3.7, \(\underline{G^0}\) is ind-constructible in S, hence \(s_i^{-1}(\underline{G^0})\) is ind-constructible in \(S_i\). It then follows from EGA IV2, 8.3.4, that there exists an index \(j\geqslant i\) such that \(s_j^{-1}(\underline{G^0}) = S_j\), where \(s_j\) is the map deduced from \(s_i\) by the base change \(S_j\to S_i\). This shows that \(s_j\in G^0(S_j)\), hence that s comes from an element of \(\varinjlim G^0(S_i)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.9

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation. Suppose that \(G^0\) is representable; then the canonical morphism \(i : G^0 \to G\) is an open immersion; moreover, \(G^0\) is quasi-compact over S.

Since \(G^0\) is a subfunctor of the functor G, the morphism \(i\) is a monomorphism, hence is radicial. From the definition of the functor \(G^0\), one checks immediately from the definition (EGA IV4, 17.1.1) that \(i\) is a formally étale morphism (remarking that \(\underline{G^0}\) is the image of \(i\) in G). Finally, it follows from the characterization (EGA IV3, 8.14.2 c)) of S-schemes locally of finite presentation by means of the functor they represent, and from 3.8, that, since G is locally of finite presentation over S, the same holds for \(G^0\). Hence \(i\) is locally of finite presentation; it is therefore a radicial and étale morphism, hence an open immersion (EGA IV4, 17.9.1). The last assertion follows from 3.6.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.10

Let G be an S-group. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    G is smooth over S at the points of the unit section.

  2. (ii)

    G is flat and locally of finite presentation over S at the points of the unit section, and for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\).

  3. (iii)

    There exists an open group subscheme G' of G that is smooth over S.

  4. (iv)

    \(G^0\) is representable by an open subscheme of G, smooth over S.
It is clear that (iv) implies (iii), that (iii) implies (i), that (i) implies (iii) (2.4) and that (i) is equivalent to (ii) (1.3.1 and EGA IV 17.5.1).

Let us finally show that (iii) implies (iv). Lemma 3.10.1 below shows that G' contains \(\underline{G^0}\) and that \(\underline{G'^0} = \underline{G^0}\). As it suffices to show that \(\underline{G^0}\) is open in G (3.4), we may assume that \(G' = G\). Then \(\pi\) is locally of finite presentation, hence, given \(s\in S\), one can construct, as in the proof of 3.7, an open subset T of S containing s, and the open set W such that the morphism \(\pi'' : W \to T\) deduced from \(\pi'\) is of finite presentation and admits as a section the morphism \(\varepsilon'' : T \to W\) deduced from \(\varepsilon\). For every \(t\in T\), \(W\cap G^0_t\) is then the connected component of \(\pi''^{-1}(t)\) containing \(\varepsilon''(t)\). Since \(\pi\) is smooth, the same holds for \(\pi''\), which is therefore reduced and of finite presentation: then (EGA IV 15.6.5), the union \(W^0\) of the \(W\cap G^0_t\) for \(t\in T\) is open in W. But it follows from (VIA 0.5) that \(W^0\cdot W^0\) is equal to \(\underline{G^0}\cap\pi^{-1}(T)\). To show that \(\underline{G^0}\) is open, it suffices to show that every \(s\in S\) has a neighborhood T in S such that \(\underline{G^0}\cap\pi^{-1}(T)\) is open in \(\pi^{-1}(T)\). We may therefore assume from now on that \(T = S\); it remains to show that \(W^0\cdot W^0\) is open in G. Since \(\pi\) is universally open, the same holds for \(\mu\) (VIA 1). Hence, since \(W^0\) is open in G, the same holds for \(W^0\cdot W^0 = \mu(W^0\times_S W^0)\).
This result will be improved in 4.4.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10.1

Let G be an S-group with fibers locally of finite type. Then every open group subscheme G' of G contains \(\underline{G^0}\), and satisfies: \(\underline{G^0} = \underline{G'^0}\).

Let \(s\in S\); set \(G''_s = G'_s\cap G^0_s\); then \(G''_s\) is an open subset of \(G^0_s\), which is dense in \(G^0_s\) since \(G^0_s\) is irreducible (VIA 4), hence \(G''_s\cdot G''_s = G^0_s\) (VIA 0.5), which shows that \(G^0_s = G''_s\cdot G''_s \subset G'_s\cdot G'_s = G'_s\), hence that \(\underline{G^0}\subset G'\). Hence \(G^0\) is a subfunctor of the functor G', and it follows from 3.2 (ii) c) that \(G'^0 = G^0\), hence \(\underline{G'^0} = \underline{G^0}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.11

Let G and H be two S-groups locally of finite presentation, and \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of S-groups; then, if u is flat, the map \(u^0 : G^0 \to H^0\) deduced from u is surjective; the converse is true if G is flat over S and if H has reduced fibers.

So suppose u flat; then for every \(s\in S\), \(u_s\) is flat and locally of finite presentation, hence open (EGA 2.4.6), hence the morphism \(u^0_s : G^0_s \to H^0_s\) is dominant. Since \(G^0_s\) is of finite type over \(\kappa(s)\) (VIA 4) and \(H_s\) is separated over \(\kappa(s)\) (VIA 2), \(u^0_s\) is quasi-compact (EGA I, 6.6.4), hence surjective (1.2). Hence \(u^0\) is surjective.

Conversely, suppose \(u^0\) surjective, G flat over S and H with reduced fibers. Then, for every \(s\in S\), \(u^0_s\) is surjective, and \(H_s\) is reduced; hence \(H^0_s\) is locally noetherian and integral, and \(u^0_s\) is of finite type, hence flat at the generic point of \(G_s\) (EGA IV 6.9.1), hence \(u_s\) is flat (1.3), so that u is flat (EGA IV 11.3.11).

pages.title: 4. Dimension of the fibers of groups locally of finite presentation

blocks.html: 4. Dimension of the fibers of groups locally of finite presentation

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1

Let G be an S-scheme locally of finite type, equipped with an S-section \(\varepsilon\), and such that for every \(s\in S\), we have \(\dim G_s = \dim_{\varepsilon(s)} G_s\) (which is the case if G is an S-group (1.5)). Then the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is upper semi-continuous on S. If, moreover, G is locally of finite presentation over S, this function is locally constructible.

Let \(\pi : G \to S\) be the structural morphism. Chevalley's theorem (EGA IV3, 13.1.3) asserts that the function \(x \mapsto \dim_x \pi^{-1}(\pi(x))\) is upper semi-continuous on G. Now, for every \(s\in S\), \(\dim G_s = \dim \pi^{-1}(s) = \dim_{\varepsilon(s)} \pi^{-1}(\pi(\varepsilon(s)))\); and since the function \(s\mapsto \varepsilon(s)\) is continuous on S, the composite function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is upper semi-continuous on S.

Suppose G locally of finite presentation over S. To show that the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is locally constructible, one sees, arguing as before, that it suffices to show that the function \(x\mapsto \dim_x \pi^{-1}(\pi(x))\) is locally constructible on G, which follows from EGA IV3, 9.9.1.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let G be an S-scheme locally of finite presentation, equipped with an S-section \(\varepsilon\) and satisfying the two following conditions:

a) For every \(s\in S\) and every \(x\in G_s\), we have \(\dim G_s = \dim_x G_s\) (or, what amounts to the same thing, for every \(s\in S\), all the irreducible components of \(G_s\) have the same dimension).

b) For every \(s\in S\), if we denote by \(G^0_s\) the connected component of \(G_s\) containing \(\varepsilon(s)\), \(G^0_s\) is geometrically irreducible.

(Recall that an S-group G satisfies conditions a) and b) (1.5 and VIA 4.1)). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    G is universally open over S at the points of \(G^0_s\).

  2. (i bis)

    G is universally open over S at the points of \(\varepsilon(s)\).

  3. (ii)

    The function \(t\mapsto \dim G_t\) is constant in a neighborhood of s in S.

Evidently (i) ⇒ (i bis). Let us show that (i bis) implies (ii). Let T be the set of the \(t\in S\) such that \(\dim G_t = \dim G_s\). By 4.1, T is locally constructible, hence (EGA IV 1.10.1), to show that T is a neighborhood of s, it suffices to show that every generization \(s'\) of s belongs to T. Now (EGA IV 14.3.13 b')), if x denotes the generic point of \(G^0_s\), we have, for every generization \(s'\) of s: \(\dim G_{s'} \geqslant \dim_x G_s = \dim G_s\); but, by (4.1), the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) being upper semi-continuous, we have \(\dim G_{s'} \leqslant \dim G_s\); hence \(s'\in T\), q.e.d.

Let us show that (ii) implies (i). Since \(\pi\) is locally of finite presentation, there exist an open subset U of G containing \(\varepsilon(s)\) and an open subset V of S containing s such that \(\pi(U)\subset V\) and such that the morphism \(\pi' : U \to V\) deduced from \(\pi\) is of finite presentation. Then set \(T = \varepsilon^{-1}(U)\) and \(W = \pi'^{-1}(T) = U\cap\pi^{-1}(T)\). Then the morphism \(\pi'' : W \to T\) deduced from \(\pi'\) is of finite presentation and admits as a section the morphism \(\varepsilon'' : T \to W\) deduced from \(\varepsilon\). Moreover, for every \(t\in T\), \(G^0_t\) being irreducible, \(W\cap G^0_t\) is dense in \(G^0_t\), hence irreducible, hence connected: it is therefore the connected component of \(\pi''^{-1}(t)\) containing \(\varepsilon''(t)\). By (EGA IV3, 10.7.1 and 10.6.2), \(W\cap G^0_t\) being a dense open subset of \(G^0_t\), we have \(\dim(W\cap G^0_t) = \dim G^0_t = \dim G_t\), hence the function \(t\mapsto \dim W\cap G^0_t\) is constant in a neighborhood of s in T. Let us finally show that, for every \(t\in T\), \(W\cap G^0_t\) is geometrically irreducible: let K be an extension of \(\kappa(t)\); then \((W\cap G^0_t)\otimes_{\kappa(t)} K = (W\otimes_{\kappa(t)} K)\cap(G^0_t\otimes_{\kappa(t)} K)\) is a nonempty open subset of \(G^0_t\otimes_{\kappa(t)} K\), which is irreducible, hence it is irreducible.

We are then in a position to apply EGA IV3, 15.6.6 (ii), which asserts that \(\pi''\) (hence \(\pi\)) is universally open at the points of \(W\cap G^0_s\). But, by EGA IV3, 14.3.3.1 (ii), the subset of \(G_s\) consisting of the points where \(\pi\) is universally open is closed in \(G_s\); since it contains \(W\cap G^0_s\), it therefore contains its closure \(G^0_s\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let G be a flat S-group locally of finite presentation. Then the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is locally constant on S.
This follows immediately from 4.2, for every flat morphism locally of finite presentation is universally open (EGA IV2, 2.4.6).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation over S at the points of the unit section. Consider the conditions:

  1. (i)

    G is smooth over S at the points of the unit section (cf. 3.10).

  2. (ii)

    For every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\), and the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is locally constant on S.

  3. (iii)

    For every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\), and there exists a neighborhood V of the unit section such that the restriction to V of the structural morphism \(\pi : G \to S\) is universally open.

  4. (iv)

    For every \(s\in S\), \(G^0_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\), and \(G^0\) is representable by an open group subscheme of G, universally open over S.

Then we have the following implications: (i) ⇒ (ii) ⇔ (iii) ⇔ (iv).

If we suppose moreover S reduced, then conditions (i) to (iv) are equivalent, and imply that \(G^0\) is smooth over S.

Let us show that (i) implies (ii). By (EGA IV 17.10.2), the function \(x\mapsto \dim_x \pi^{-1}(\pi(x)) = \dim\pi^{-1}(\pi(x))\) (1.5) is continuous in a neighborhood of the unit section; hence the function \(s\mapsto \dim G_s\) is continuous on S, hence locally constant on S. On the other hand, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\) (1.3.1).

Let us show that (ii) implies (iv). The question is local on S, for it suffices to show that \(\underline{G^0}\) is open in G (3.4), the properties of \(G^0\) cited in the statement then following from (2.4, 4.2). Given \(s\in S\), construct as in the proof of (3.10), W, T, \(\pi''\), \(\varepsilon''\) and \(W^0\). It then follows from EGA IV3, 15.6.7, that \(W^0\) is open in W. It follows from (4.2) that \(\pi\) is universally open at every point of \(W^0\), hence (VIA 1) \(\mu\) is universally open at every point of \(W^0\times_S W^0\), which shows that \(W^0\cdot W^0\) is open, and one concludes as in the proof of theorem 3.10.
It is clear that (iv) implies (iii).
The fact that (iii) implies (ii) follows from (4.2) applied to V.

Let us finally show that if S is reduced, (iv) implies (i): we may evidently assume \(G = G^0\), hence G is of finite presentation over S by virtue of (5.4) below, and the conclusion then follows from (EGA IV3, 15.6.7) (implying that G is flat over S) and from (3.10).

pages.title: 5. Separation of groups and homogeneous spaces

blocks.html: 5. Separation of groups and homogeneous spaces

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1

For an S-group G to be separated, it is necessary and sufficient that the unit section of G be a closed immersion.

The condition is necessary (EGA I, 5.4.6); it is sufficient by virtue of the following cartesian diagram (cf. VIA 3): \[ \xymatrix{ G\times_S G \ar[r]^-{\mu\circ(\mathrm{id}_G\times c)} & G \\ G \ar[u]^-{\Delta_{G/S}} \ar[r]_-{\pi} & S \ar[u]_-{\varepsilon} } \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2

If S is discrete, every S-group is separated.

Indeed, S is then equal to \(\coprod_{s\in S}\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\), and it suffices to show that for every \(s\in S\), \(G|\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) is separated (EGA I 5.5.5), which follows from VIA 2, since \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) is an artinian local ring.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3

Let S be a scheme, G an S-group scheme locally of finite presentation over S and universally open along the unit section, H an S-scheme on which G acts in such a way that the morphism: \[ G\times_S H \longrightarrow H\times_S H, \qquad (g,h)\longmapsto (gh,h) \] is surjective (intuitively, H is a homogeneous space under G). Suppose moreover that for every \(s\in S\), there exists an open subscheme U of H, separated over S, such that \(U_s\) is dense in \(H_s\). Then H is separated over S.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Let S, G, H be as above and suppose moreover H with connected fibers; then X is separated over S and quasi-compact over S.

Indeed, since \(H_s\) is connected, \(H_s\) is also irreducible (if it is nonempty), so that if U is an affine open subset of H such that \(U_s\) is nonempty, \(U_s\) is dense in \(H_s\), and the theorem applies.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let S be a scheme, G an S-group scheme, locally of finite presentation, with connected fibers, and universally open over S. Then G is separated and of finite presentation over S.
Indeed, we have just seen that G is separated over S, hence quasi-separated. On the other hand, G is quasi-compact over S by 3.6.
Proof of 5.3. Before establishing 5.3, let us prove a few lemmas. The following lemma advantageously replaces EGA IV3, 19.5.10 in that it is independent of noetherian hypotheses.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.6.1

Let S be a scheme, \(f : X \to S\) an S-scheme locally of finite presentation, s a point of S, x a closed point of \(X_s\), z the generic point of an irreducible component Z of \(X_s\) containing x such that \(\dim_x(X_s) = \dim Z\), and suppose that f is universally open at z (hence also at x). Then there exist a morphism \(g : S'' \to S' \to S\) with \(S'\to S\) étale of finite presentation covering s, \(S''\to S'\) finite, of finite presentation, surjective, such that \(g^{-1}(s)\) consists of a single point \(s''\), and an S-morphism \(h : S'' \to X\) such that \(h(s'') = x\).

It is clear that we may assume X affine of finite presentation over S, then by passage to the limit replace S by \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\), then assume S strictly henselian, with closed point s. Let then \(n = \dim_x X_s\). The choice of a system of parameters of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_s,x}\) and the "Main Theorem" then show that, after shrinking X if necessary, there exists a quasi-finite S-morphism of finite presentation \(u : X \to S[T_1,\ldots,T_n] = Y\). Moreover, \(u(x)\) is a closed point of \(Y_s\) and we may assume that \(u^{-1}(u(x))\) has underlying space consisting of a single point. Let V be the subscheme of X that is the inverse image under u of a section of Y over S passing through \(u(x)\). Hence V is quasi-finite over S and contains x as the unique point above s. Since S is henselian, \(V = V'\coprod V''\), with V' finite over S and V'' above \(S-s\), so that V' is the spectrum of a local ring. Let us show that \(V'\to S\) is surjective. For this, we may replace S by the reduced closed subscheme underlying an irreducible component of S, then assume S normal, hence geometrically unibranch. Since f is universally open at z, there exists an irreducible component \(\mathfrak{z}\) of X containing z and equidimensional over S (of relative dimension n) at the point z. Since u is quasi-finite and Y of relative dimension n, necessarily \(\mathfrak{z}\) dominates Y. Again because u is quasi-finite, \(\mathfrak{z}\) will also be equidimensional over Y at the point x. Since \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,u(x)}\) is geometrically unibranch (EGA IV3, 14.4.1.1.), Chevalley's theorem tells us that u is universally open at x, hence V and also V' are universally open over S at x; a fortiori, V' dominates S.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6.2

Let S, G, H, s be as in the theorem, U an open subscheme of H such that \(U_s\) is dense in \(H_s\), and let T be a subset of \(H_s\). Then there exist a morphism \(g : S'' \to S' \to S\) as in lemma 1 and an element \(b\in G(S'')\) such that \(g^{-1}(T)\) is contained in \(b\cdot U\).

After shrinking S if necessary, we may assume that there exists an open neighborhood V of the unit section of G which is of finite presentation and universally open over S. We shall construct b in \(V(S'')\). One then sees that we may further replace S by the spectrum of a strict henselization of \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\). We may assume the \(a_i\in T\) closed in \(H_s\). If some of the points \(a_i\) have residue fields that are nontrivial (necessarily finite radicial) extensions of \(\kappa(s)\), then after performing a finite sequence of flat, finite extensions of finite presentation, of the type \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\to\mathscr{O}_{S,s}[T]/(T^p - x)\) (\(x\in\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\)), we see that we may assume the \(a_i\) rational. The hypothesis made on H implies that there exist, for \(i=0,1,\ldots,n\), closed points \(b'_i\) of \(G_s\times X_s\) which are sent, by the canonical morphism, to the points \((a_i,a_0)\) of \(X_s\times X_s\). Proceeding as above, we may assume the \(b'_i\) rational, hence of the form \((b_i,h_0)\), where \(b_i\in G_s(\kappa(s))\) is such that \(a_i = b_i\cdot a_0\). Let then \(W_s\) be the open subset of \(G_s\) that is the inverse image of \(U_s\) under the morphism \(b\mapsto b\cdot a_0\). The hypothesis made on \(U_s\) implies that \(W_s\) is dense in \(G_s\). If \(b_s\) is a rational point of \(G_s\), we then have the following equivalences: \[ a_i\in b_s\cdot U_s \Longleftrightarrow b_s^{-1}b_i\cdot a_0\in U_s \Longleftrightarrow b_s^{-1}b_i \in W_s \Longleftrightarrow b_s\in b_i W_s^{-1}. \] Since \(W_s\) is dense in \(G_s\), the same holds for the \(b_i W_s^{-1}\) and for their intersection. We can therefore find a closed point x of \(V_s\cap[\cap_i b_i W_s^{-1}]\). After performing a surjective finite extension of finite presentation, we may assume, by lemma 1, that there exists a section b of G passing through x. It is clear that the section b answers the question.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.6.3

Let X be an S-scheme. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    X is separated over S.

  2. (ii)

    For every S-scheme T, every section \(h : T \to X_T\) is a closed immersion.

  3. (iii)

    For every reduced S-scheme T, two morphisms \(f_1\) and \(f_2 : T \to X\) which coincide on a dense open subset U of T coincide.

  4. (iv)

    For every S-scheme T, every point \(t\in T\) and every pair of rational points \(x_1, x_2\) of \(X_t = X\times_S \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(t)\), there exist a morphism \(g : T'' \to T' \to T\) with \(T'\to T\) open, \(T''\to T'\) closed dominant, such that \(g^{-1}(t)\neq\varnothing\), and finally an open subscheme V of \(X_{T''}\), separated over \(T''\), containing \(g^{-1}(x_1)\) and \(g^{-1}(x_2)\).
(i)⇒(iv): take \(T'' = T\) and \(V = X_T\).

(iv)⇒(iii): we may assume \(T = S\). Since T is reduced, to see that \(f_1 = f_2\), it then suffices to see that \(f_1 = f_2\) set-theoretically. So let \(t\in T\) and let us show that \(f_1(t) = f_2(t)\). Let \(g : T'' \to T' \to T\) be as in (iv). Since U is dense in T and \(T'\to T\) is open, the inverse image U' of U in T' is dense in T'. Let U'' be the inverse image of U' in T'' and \(\overline{U''}\) its closure in T''. Since \(T''\to T'\) is closed dominant, \(\overline{U''}\to T'\) is surjective, hence \(\overline{U''}\wedge g^{-1}(t)\) is nonempty. It is clear then that we may replace U and T by the reduced subschemes of T'' having U'' and \(\overline{U''}\) as underlying spaces, and replace \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) by their inverse images under the base change \(\overline{U''}\to T\). In short, we may assume that there exists an open subset V of \(X_T\), separated over T, containing \(f_1(t)\) and \(f_2(t)\). Let \(W = f_1^{-1}(V)\cap f_2^{-1}(V)\); it is an open subset of T containing t, and \(U\cap W\) is dense in W. We may therefore replace T by W. But then it is clear that \(f_1 = f_2\), hence \(f_1(t) = f_2(t)\).

(iii)⇒(ii). We may assume T reduced. Let E be the subscheme \(h(T)\) of \(X_T\) and let \(\overline{E}\) be the reduced closed subscheme of \(X_T\) having the closure of E as underlying space, so that E is a dense open subscheme of \(\overline{E}\). After the base change \(\overline{E}\to S\), we have two sections of \(X_{\overline{E}}\) which coincide on E, hence they coincide, and consequently \(E = \overline{E}\).
(ii)⇒(i). Take \(T = X\) and for h the diagonal section.
The theorem then follows trivially from lemma 2 iv)⇒i) and from the corollary to lemma 1.

blocks.title: Counterexample
blocks.html:
Counterexample 5.6.4

Not every S-group G is separated. Let S be a scheme having a non-isolated closed point s; let G be the scheme obtained by gluing two copies of S along the open set \(S-\{s\}\); one easily sees that G is not separated over S, and that it is equipped with a natural group structure all of whose fibers are trivial, except the fiber \(G_s\), which is isomorphic to the two-element group \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6.5

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation, with connected fibers, and universally open over S; then G is of finite presentation over S.
This follows from 5.3 and 3.6.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.6.6

A proof analogous to that of theorem 5.3, but using 4.7, allows one to establish the following result:

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation and universally open over S at the points of \(\underline{G^0}\). Let \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\) be two S-sections of \(G^0\) (i.e. let \(\sigma,\tau\in G^0(S)\)). Then the subscheme of S of coincidences of \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\) is closed.
6. Subfunctors and group subschemes(*)

(*) On this same theme, see also the results of XI 6, whose natural place would be in the present exposé VIB. There one will find in particular representability criteria for certain group subfunctors of a given group scheme.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

(i) Let X be an S-functor (that is, a functor from \((\mathrm{Sch}_{/S})^{\circ}\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\)), G an S-group functor, u and v two S-morphisms from X to G. We call the transporter of u into v, denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(u,v)\), the S-subfunctor T of G defined by \(T(S') = \{g\in G(S') \mid (\mathrm{int}\,g)\circ u_{S'} = v_{S'}\}\).

(ii) Let G be an S-group functor, X and Y two S-subfunctors of G; we call the transporter of X into Y (resp. strict transporter of X into Y), denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(X,Y)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,Y)\)), the S-subfunctor T of G defined by \(T(S') = \{g\in G(S') \mid (\mathrm{int}\,g)(X_{S'}) \subset Y_{S'}\}\) (resp. \((\mathrm{int}\,g)(X_{S'}) = Y_{S'}\)). Note that we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,Y) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(X,Y)\cap c(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Y,X)), \] where c denotes the inverse map of G.

(iii) Let G be an S-group functor, X an S-functor, u an S-morphism from X to G; we call the centralizer of u the S-group subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(u) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(u,u)\) of G.

(iv) Let G be an S-group functor, H an S-subfunctor of G, i the canonical S-morphism \(H\to G\); we call the centralizer (resp. normalizer of H in G) the S-group subfunctor of G \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G H = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(i) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(i,i)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H = \underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(H,H)\)). Finally, we call the center of G the S-group functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}\,G = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G G\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.2

Let G be an S-group. Consider, for a subfunctor T of the functor G, the following property:

(+f) for every \(s\in S\), \(T_s\) is representable by a closed subscheme of \(G_s\).

  1. (i)

    Let X be an S-scheme and u and v two S-morphisms from X to G. Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(u,v)\) satisfies condition (+f).

  2. (ii)

    Let \(u : X \to G\) and \(v : Y \to G\) be two monomorphisms of schemes;

    (1) if, for every \(s\in S\), \(v_s\) is a closed immersion, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(X,Y)\) satisfies condition (+f);

    (2) if, for every \(s\in S\), \(u_s\) and \(v_s\) are closed immersions, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,Y)\) satisfies condition (+f).

  3. (iii)

    Let \(u : H \to G\) be a monomorphism; then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G H\) satisfies condition (+f); if, moreover, for every \(s\in S\), \(u_s\) is a closed immersion, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H\) satisfies condition (+f).

  4. (iv)

    Let \(u : X \to G\) be a morphism; then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(u)\) satisfies condition (+f).

It suffices to prove (i) and (ii); assertion (i) follows from VIII 6.5 b)(*) applied to the S-morphisms \(q_1, q_2 : G \rightrightarrows \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,G)\) defined by \(q_1(g) = (\mathrm{int}\,g)\circ u\) and \(q_2(g) = v\), taking into account that for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is separated (VIA 2). Assertion (ii) follows from VIII 6.5 e).

(*) The reader will observe that the developments of (VIII no. 6 and of XI no. 6) do not depend on the earlier sections of those exposés, nor on exposés VI and VII, and could have come as early as the present exposé, where their natural place was.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.3

Let k be an algebraically closed field, G a k-group and H a group subscheme of G; suppose G and H of finite type over k and reduced; then \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G H\)) is representable by a group subscheme of G, whose associated reduced subscheme is none other than the normalizer (resp. the centralizer) of H in G in the sense of Bible.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.4

Let G be an S-group, and \(u : X \to G\) a monomorphism of S-schemes. Set \(T = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(X,X)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    T is a group subfunctor of G.

  2. (ii)

    \(T = \underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,X) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G X\).

These conditions are satisfied in each of the two following cases:

a) X is of finite presentation over S.

b) T is representable by a scheme of finite presentation over S.

The equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) follows from the fact that, for every morphism \(S'\to S\) and all \(t,t'\in T(S')\), we have \(t\cdot t'\in T(S')\), and from the fact that \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,X) = T\cap c(T)\) (cf. 6.1 (ii)).

Let us consider case a). Let \(t\in T(S)\); then \(\mathrm{int}(t)\) is a monomorphism from X to X, hence an S-automorphism of X (EGA IV4, 17.9.6), so that t belongs to \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(X,X)\), whence a).
In case b), it is clear that \(\mu(T\times_S T)\subset T\), and the assertion follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4.2

Let G be an S-scheme of finite presentation, equipped with an associative law (in the sense of EGA 0III 8.2.5). Suppose that for every S-scheme S' and every \(g\in G(S')\), the right and left translations by g in the set \(G(S')\) are injective, and that \(G(S)\neq\varnothing\). Then G is an S-group.

It suffices to show that, for every S-scheme S', the set \(G(S')\) is a group; now, from the hypothesis it follows at once that the right and left translations by any element \(g\in G(S')\) in \(G_{S'}\) are S-monomorphisms from \(G_{S'}\) to \(G_{S'}\). They are therefore S-automorphisms, since G is of finite presentation over S (EGA IV4, 17.9.6), so that the right and left translations by g in the set \(G(S')\) are bijective, and we are reduced to the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4.3

Let G be a nonempty set equipped with an associative law such that the right and left translations are bijective. Then G is a group.
The proof is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.5

Let G be an S-group and let \(u : H \to G\) be a monomorphism. We call the connected centralizer of H in G, denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}^0_G H\), the identity component of the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G H\) (cf. 3.1 and 6.2 (iv)). When for every \(s\in S\), \(u_s\) is a closed immersion (which is the case when H is an S-group scheme, G and H are locally of finite type over S and u is a quasi-compact S-morphism of groups (1.4.2)), we call the connected normalizer of H in G, denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}^0_G H\), the identity component of the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H\). Given a morphism \(u : X \to G\), one defines likewise the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}^0(u)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.5.1

Let G be an S-group locally of finite presentation and quasi-separated over S, H a smooth S-group with connected fibers and \(u : H \to G\) a monomorphism. Let N be the normalizer of H in G (cf. I or 6.1). We shall see (XI 6.11)(*) that N is representable by a closed group subscheme of G, of finite presentation over G. This being so, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The canonical morphism \(H\to N\) is an open immersion.

  2. (ii)

    \(N^0 = H\) (cf. 3.10).

  3. (iii)

    For every \(s\in S\), we have: \(H_s = (N_s)^0\).
(*) See the note (*) on the preceding page.
Condition (i) implies (ii) by lemma 3.10.1, since \(H^0 = H\). On the other hand, it is clear that (ii) implies (iii), for \(H_s = (N^0)_s = (N_s)^0\).

Let us finally show that (iii) implies (i). Since \(H_s = (N^0)_s\) for every \(s\in S\), \(H_s \to N_s\) is an open immersion. Moreover, H and N are locally of finite presentation over S, and H is flat over S, hence (EGA IV4, 17.9.5) \(H\to N\) is an open immersion.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.6
