Let G be an S-group functor, H an S-group subfunctor; we say that H is normal (resp. central, resp. characteristic) in G if \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H = G\) (resp. if \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G H = G\), resp. if, for every S-scheme T and every automorphism \(a\in \mathrm{Aut}_{T\text{-gr.}}(G_T)\), we have: \(a(H_T)\subset H_T\)); in other words, if, for every S-scheme T, the subgroup H(T) of G(T) is normal in G(T) (resp. central in G(T), resp. invariant under every automorphism of \(G_T\)).
If H is central (resp. characteristic), it is normal.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.7

Let G and H be two S-groups and \(u : H \to G\) a monomorphism. For H to be normal (resp. central, resp. characteristic) in G, it is necessary and sufficient that the morphism \[ \mu\circ\bigl(c\circ\mathrm{pr}_2,\ \mu\circ(u\times\mathrm{id}_G)\bigr) : H\underset{S}{\times} G \longrightarrow G \] (defined by \((h,g)\mapsto g^{-1}\cdot h\) for all \(g\in G(S')\) and \(h\in H(S')\)) factor through u (resp. be equal to \(u\circ\mathrm{pr}_1\), resp. that for every S-scheme T and every T-group automorphism \(a\) of \(G\times_S T\), \(a\circ u(T)\) factor through \(u(T)\)).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 6.8

Let G be an S-group functor. Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}\,G\) is characteristic and central. If, for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is representable, \(G^0\) is characteristic. This follows from the definitions and from 3.3.
7. Generated subgroups; commutator group
In this section, k denotes a fixed field.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.1

Let G be a k-group, \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) a family of separable k-schemes (EGA IV 4.6.2); for every \(i\in I\), let \(f_i : X_i \to G\) be a k-morphism.

  1. (i)

    There exists a smallest closed k-subgroup scheme of G dominating each of the \(f_i\), say \(\Gamma_G((f_i)_{i\in I})\). It is a separable k-scheme, hence smooth in the case where G is assumed locally of finite type over k.

  2. (ii)

    Set \(X = \coprod_{i\in I} X_i\), and let \(f : X \to G\) be the morphism whose restriction to \(X_i\) is \(f_i\), for every \(i\in I\). Set \(X^1 = X\coprod X\), and let \(f^1 : X^1 \to G\) be the morphism whose restrictions to X are respectively f and \(c\circ f\). For every \(n>1\), set \(X^n = X^1\times_k X^{n-1}\) and \(f^n = \mu\circ(f^1\times_k f^{n-1}) : X^n \to G\). Then \(\Gamma_G((f_i)_{i\in I})\) is the reduced subscheme of G having as underlying space the closure of the union of the \(f^n(X^n)\), for \(n>1\).

  3. (iii)

    For every k-scheme S, \(\Gamma_G((f_i)_{i\in I})_S\) is the smallest closed group subscheme of \(G_S\) dominating each of the \(f_{i,S} : X_{i,S} \to G_S\). If we suppose that G is locally of finite type over k, then \(\Gamma_G((f_i)_{i\in I})_S\) is the smallest group subscheme of \(G_S\) dominating each of the \(f_{i,S}\).
First note that, to prove (i) and (iii), by defining X and f as in (ii), we are reduced to the case where I consists of a single element.

Let H be the reduced subscheme of G with underlying set \(\overline{\bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} f^n(X^n)}\). Then H is separable (EGA IV 11.10.7). It is clear that every closed subscheme of G that dominates f also dominates H. Hence, to prove (i) and (ii), it suffices to show that H is a group subscheme of G, hence that the restriction of c to H and the restriction of \(\mu\) to \(H\times_k H\) factor through the injection \(H\to G\). Since H is separable, \(H\times_k H\) is reduced, and it suffices to check that \(c(H)\subset H\) and that \(\mu(H\times_k H)\subset H\) (set-theoretically). But by EGA IV3, 11.10.6, the union of the \(f^n_{(H)}(X^n\times_k H)\) is dense in \(H\times_k H\). Likewise, for every \(n\geqslant 1\), the union of the \(f^m_{(X^n)}(X^n\times_k X^m)\), for \(m\geqslant 1\), is dense in \(X^n\times_k H\). Hence it suffices to show that \(\mu(f^n_{(H)}(f^m_{(X^n)}(X^n\times_k X^m)))\subset H\) and that \(c(f^n(X^n))\subset H\). Now \(\mu(f^n_{(H)}(f^m_{(X^n)}(X^n\times_k X^m))) = \mu((f^n\times_k f^m)(X^n\times_k X^m)) = f^{n+m}(X^{n+m})\subset H\); and, since \(c(f^1(X^1))\subset f^1(X^1)\), we have, for every n, \(c(f^n(X^n))\subset f^n(X^n)\subset H\). This proves (i) and (ii).

Let us now prove (iii). Let G' be a closed group subscheme of \(G_S\) dominating \(f_S\); we must show that G' dominates \(H_S\), or, what amounts to the same thing, that \(H_S = H_S\times_{G_S} G'\). Set \(H'_S = H_S\times_{G_S} G' = G'\cap H_S\). Since G' and \(H_S\) both dominate the \(f^n_S\), the same holds for \(H'_S\). Now (EGA IV3, 11.10.6), since the family of the \(f^n : X^n \to H\) is schematically dominant, the same holds for the family of the \(f^n_S : X^n_S \to H_S\), so that \(H'_S\), which dominates each of the \(f^n_S\), is equal to \(H_S\) (EGA IV3, 11.10.1 c)). Let us now prove the second assertion of (iii) in the case where G is locally of finite type over k. Let G' be a group subscheme of \(G_S\) dominating \(f_S\). We must likewise show that, if we set \(H'_S = H_S\times_{G_S} G'\), we have \(H'_S = H_S\). For this it suffices to show that \(H'_S\) is closed in \(H_S\) and to apply the preceding argument. It therefore suffices to show that \(H_S\) and \(H'_S\) have the same underlying set; a fortiori, it suffices to show that, for every \(s\in S\), \(H_{S,s} = H'_{S,s}\). But then \(H'_{S,s} = G'_S\times_{\kappa(s)} H_{S,s}\); and \(G'_s\), being a \(\kappa(s)\)-group subscheme of \(G_{S,s'}\) is closed (1.4), hence \(H'_{S,s}\) is closed in \(H_{S,s}\); and then the argument from the beginning, applied to \(H'_{S,s}\), to \(H_{S,s}\) and to the \(f^n_{S,s}\), shows that \(H'_{S,s} = H_{S,s}\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Definition and remark
blocks.html:
Definition and remark 7.2

(i) Given a k-group G, a family \((X_i)_{i\in I}\) of separable k-schemas and, for each \(i\in I\), a k-morphism \(f_i : X_i \to G\), we call the closed group subscheme of G generated by the family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\), and we shall denote in this section by \(\Gamma_G((f_i)_{i\in I})\), the smallest closed group subscheme of G dominating each of the \(f_i\). In the case where \(i\) is the injection of a subscheme X of G separable over k, one writes \(\Gamma_G(X)\) instead of \(\Gamma_G(i)\).

(ii) It is clear that if \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are two separable k-schemes and \(f_1 : X_1 \to G\) and \(f_2 : X_2 \to G\) two k-morphisms such that the sets \(\overline{f_1(X_1)}\) and \(\overline{f_2(X_2)}\) are equal, then \(\Gamma_G(f_1) = \Gamma_G(f_2)\).

(iii) Let E be a subset of G such that the reduced subscheme \(\overline{E}\) of G is separable. We call the closed group subscheme of G generated by E, denoted \(\Gamma_G(E)\), the group subscheme \(\Gamma_G(i)\), where i is the injection of the reduced subscheme \(\overline{E}\) of G into G.

(iv) In the case where G is locally of finite type over k, every group subscheme of G being closed (1.4), one will speak of the "generated group subscheme" instead of the "generated closed group subscheme".

(v) Let X be a separable k-scheme and \(f : X \to G\) a k-morphism. Suppose that \(f(X)\) contains the unit element e of G. Set \(X'^1 = X\times_k X\) and \(f'^1 = \mu\circ(f\times_k(c\circ f))\), and for \(n>1\), \(X'^n = X'^1\times_k X'^{n-1}\) and \(f'^n = \mu\circ(f'^1\times_k f'^{n-1})\). Then \(\Gamma_G(f)\) is the reduced subscheme of G whose underlying space is the closure of the union, for \(n\geqslant 1\), of the \(f'^n(X'^n)\). Indeed, since \(f(X)\) contains e, we have: \(f(X)\subset f'^1(X'^1)\subset f^2(X^2)\) (notation of 7.1 (ii)). Since we also have \(f^1(X^1)\subset f'^1(X'^1)\subset f^2(X^2)\), for there to exist an integer n such that \(f^n(X^n) = \Gamma_G(f)\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an integer m such that \(f'^m(X'^m) = \Gamma_G(f)\).

(vi) It follows from the preceding remark that, if X is a separable and geometrically connected k-scheme, and if \(f : X \to G\) is a k-morphism such that \(f(X)\) contains the identity element of G, then the k-group \(\Gamma_G(f)\) is connected. Indeed, each of the \(X'^n\) is then connected, so that the union of the \(f'^n(X'^n)\) (each of which contains e) is connected; hence the same holds for its closure \(\Gamma_G(f)\).

(vii) Let A and B be two separable k-subgroup schemes of a k-group G. We call the group subscheme of commutators of A and B in G, denoted \((A,B)_G\) or simply \((A,B)\), the closed group subscheme of G generated by the morphism \(\nu : A\times_k B \to G\) defined by \((a,b)\mapsto a\cdot b\cdot a^{-1}\cdot b^{-1}\) for \(a\in A(S)\) and \(b\in B(S)\). When G is separable over k, we call the derived group of G, denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Der}}(G)\), the k-group \((G,G)\). For a separable k-group G to be commutative, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\underline{\mathrm{Der}}(G)\) be the unit k-group.

(viii) We shall occasionally set \(\Gamma'_G = \bigcup_{n>1} f^n(X^n)\) (with the notations of 7.1 (ii)). Note that \(\Gamma'_G(f)\) is a subset of G stable under the group law (in the sense of 3.0).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.3

Let G be a k-group, X a separable k-scheme, \(f : X \to G\) a k-morphism. Let S be a k-scheme.

(i) Let \(u\subset \mathrm{End}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G_S)\) be such that we have the inclusion of presheaves \(u(f_S(X_S))\subset f_S(X_S)\) (where \(f_S(X_S)\) denotes the subpresheaf of \(G_S\) that is the image of \(X_S\) under \(f_S\)). Then we have \(u((\Gamma_G(f))_S)\subset (\Gamma_G(f))_S\) (as presheaves).

(i bis) Let \(u\in \mathrm{Aut}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G_S)\) be such that \(u(f_S(X_S)) = f_S(X_S)\) (as presheaves); then we have \(u((\Gamma_G(f))_S) = (\Gamma_G(f))_S\) (as presheaves).

(ii) Let \(u\in \mathrm{End}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G_S)\) be such that the restriction of u to the presheaf \(f_S(X_S)\) is the identity. Then the restriction of u to the scheme \(\Gamma_G(f)_S\) is the identity automorphism.

(iii) Let Y be a separable k-scheme and \(g : Y \to G\) a k-morphism. Suppose that, for every k-scheme S', for every \(a\in X(S')\) and \(f\in Y(S')\), \(f\circ a\) and \(g\circ b\) commute (resp. \((\mathrm{int}(f\circ a))(g_{S'}(Y_{S'})) = g_{S'}(Y_{S'})\)). Then we have \(\Gamma_G(f) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\) (resp. \(\Gamma_G(f)\subset \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\)) (as presheaves).

(iv) Let x be a point of G rational over k. Then the k-group \(\Gamma_G(\{x\})\) is commutative.

(v) Let A and B be two group subschemes of G separable over k. If A and B are normal (resp. characteristic), so is (A,B).

Let us prove (i). Set \(H = \Gamma_G(f)_S\) and \(H' = u^{-1}(H)\). By 7.1 (iii), it suffices to show that \(f_S\) factors through H'. By hypothesis, \(u\circ f_S\) belongs to \(u(f_S(X_S))(X)\), hence to \((f_S(X_S))(X)\), hence a fortiori to \(H(X)\), which means that \(u\circ f_S\) factors through the canonical injection of H into \(G_S\), hence that \(f_S\) factors through H', q.e.d.
It is clear that (i bis) is a consequence of (i).

Let us prove (ii). Since G is separated over k (VIA 0.2), \(G_S\) is separated over S, hence the unit section of \(G_S\) is a closed immersion (5.1), so that \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mu\circ(u,c))\) is a closed group subscheme of \(G_S\) (\(\mu\) (resp. c) denoting the multiplication (resp. the inverse map) of \(G_S\)). By hypothesis, the presheaf \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mu\circ(u,c))\) contains the presheaf \(f_S(X_S)\), hence \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mu\circ(u,c))\) contains \(\Gamma_G(f)_S\) (7.1 (iii)).

Let us prove (iii). Recall (6.2 (iii)) that \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\)) is representable by a closed group subscheme of G. By hypothesis, taking into account (ii) (resp. (i)), \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G \Gamma_G(g)\)) contains \(f(X)\) as a presheaf; it therefore contains \(\Gamma_G(f)\), q.e.d.

Assertion (iv) is a special case of (iii), where one takes for f and g the injection into G of the reduced scheme of G having as underlying space the closed point \(\{x\}\). This subscheme is separable since \(\kappa(x) = k\).

Let us finally prove (v). Let S be a k-scheme, and u an inner automorphism (resp. an automorphism of S-groups) of \(G_S\). By hypothesis, \(u(A_S) = A_S\) and \(u(B_S) = B_S\); one easily deduces, with the notations of (7.2 (vii)), that \(u(\nu_S(A_S\times_S B_S)) = \nu_S(A_S\times_S B_S)\); hence, by (i), we have \(u((A,B)) = (A,B)\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.4

Let G be a k-group locally of finite type, X a separable k-scheme of finite type, and let \(f : X \to G\) be a k-morphism. Suppose that X is geometrically connected and that \(f(X)\) contains the identity element e of G. Then (notations of 7.1 (ii)), there exists an integer N such that: \[ f^N(X^N) = \Gamma_G(f) \qquad (\text{set-theoretically}). \]

By 7.1 (iii) and EGA IV2, 2.6.1, we may assume k algebraically closed. By remark (7.2 (vi)), we may assume that \(G = G^0\); finally, by remark (7.2 (v)), it suffices to show that there exists an integer N such that (notations of (7.2 (v))) we have: \(f'^N(X'^N) = \Gamma_G(f)\).

First case. Suppose X irreducible. Then the \(\overline{f'^n(X'^n)}\) form an increasing sequence of irreducible closed sets in the space G, which is noetherian, since \(G = G^0\) is of finite type over k (VIA 2.4). Hence this sequence is stationary, and there exists an integer m such that \(\overline{f'^m(X'^m)} = \Gamma_G(f)\). Moreover, since X and G are of finite type over k, the \(f'^n\) are of finite type over k. Consequently, \(f'^m(X'^m)\) is constructible in G (EGA IV1, 1.8.5), hence contains an open subset U of its closure \(\Gamma_G(f)\) (EGA 0IV, 9.2.3). Then (VIA 0.5), we have \(\Gamma_G(f)\subset U\cdot U \subset f'^{2m}(X'^{2m})\subset \Gamma_G(f)\), so that \(f'^{2m}(X'^{2m}) = \Gamma_G(f)\).

Second case. Suppose that X has two irreducible components \(A_1\) and \(A_2\). Then there exists \(a\in(A_1\cap A_2)(k)\), since X is connected. Hence the four irreducible subsets \(A_i\times_k A_j\) (\(i,j=1,2\)) cover \(X\times_k X\), and the image of each of them under \(f'^1\) contains e. If \(f'^1_{ij}\) denotes the restriction of \(f'^1\) to the reduced subscheme \(A_i\times_k A_j\), set \[ Y = (A_1\underset{k}{\times}A_1)\underset{k}{\times}(A_1\underset{k}{\times}A_2)\underset{k}{\times}(A_2\underset{k}{\times}A_2)\underset{k}{\times}(A_2\underset{k}{\times}A_1) \] and \[ g = \mu\circ\Bigl((\mu\circ(f'^1_{11}\underset{k}{\times}f'^1_{12}))\underset{k}{\times}(\mu\circ(f'^1_{22}\underset{k}{\times}f'^1_{21}))\Bigr). \] Then Y is irreducible, reduced and of finite type, so we have just seen that there exists an integer m such that \(g'^m(Y'^m) = \Gamma_G(g)\). But in fact \(f'^1(X'^1)\subset g(Y)\subset f'^4(X'^4)\), so that \(\Gamma_G(f) = \Gamma_G(g)\) and \(f'^{4m}(X'^{4m}) = \Gamma_G(f)\).

General case. We argue by induction on the number of irreducible components of X (this number is finite since X, being of finite type over k, is noetherian). Suppose the proposition proved in the case where X has at most \(r-1\) irreducible components, and suppose it has r of them, namely \(A_1,\ldots,A_r\). Then e belongs to one of the \(A_i\); suppose, for example, that \(e\in A_1\). Then set \(Y = \Gamma_G(f_r)\), where \(f_r\) denotes the restriction of f to the reduced subscheme \(A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_{r-1}\) (we assume the numbering of the \(A_i\) chosen so that this scheme is connected, which is always possible). Set \(Z = \overline{f(A_r)}\). Then Y is an irreducible, reduced and closed subgroup of G (7.2 (vi)). Let then \(T = Y\cup Z\) and i the injection of the reduced closed subscheme T of G. It is clear (7.2 (ii)) that \(\Gamma_G(i) = \Gamma_G(f)\) and that T is connected (for, since X is connected, \(A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_{r-1}\) and \(A_r\) have a point a in common, and Y and Z have the point \(f(a)\) in common). Moreover, \(e\in T\), and T has at most two irreducible components, since Y and Z are irreducible. By the induction hypothesis, there exists an integer m such that, if we denote by \(X_r\) the reduced subscheme \(A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_{r-1}\) of X, we have: \(f'^m_r(X'^m_r) = \Gamma_G(f_r) = Y\). Hence, since \(X'_r\subset X\) and \(Z\subset \overline{f(X)}\), we have \(f(X)\subset Y\cup Z\subset f'^m(X'^m)\). On the other hand, since T has at most two irreducible components, we have already seen that there exists an integer p such that \(i'^p(T'^p) = \Gamma_G(i)\). Now \(\Gamma_G(i) = \Gamma_G(f)\), and \(T\subset \overline{f'^m(X'^m)}\), hence \(\overline{f'^{mp}(X'^{mp})} = \Gamma_G(f)\), and one shows, as in the first case, that this last equality implies \(f'^{2mp}(X'^{2mp}) = \Gamma_G(f)\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.5

Let S be a scheme, G an S-group scheme, X an S-prescheme, \(f : X \to G\) an S-morphism. Suppose X and G locally of finite presentation over S, and that for every \(s\in S\) and every maximal point g of \(G_s\), there exists a point x of X such that \(f(x) = g\) and f is flat at x. Then the morphism \(\mu\circ(f\times_S f) : X\times_S X \to G\) is covering for the (fppf) topology.

Note that the set V of the points of X at which f is flat is open and that \(f|V\) is an open morphism (EGA IV 11.3.1); hence, replacing X by V if necessary, we may assume f flat. It will therefore suffice to prove that the same holds for \(U\times_S U \to G\). Now, \(G\to S\) being locally of finite presentation and flat, the same holds for \(\mu : G\times_S G \to G\) (which is isomorphic to a morphism deduced from the preceding one \(G\to S\) by the base change \(G\to S\), cf. VIA 0.1), hence also for the induced morphism \(U\times_S U \to G\). To prove that it is surjective, it suffices to look fiber by fiber, where this follows from VIA 0.5.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.6

Let G be a k-group locally of finite type, X a separable k-scheme locally of finite type, and \(f : X \to G\) a k-morphism. For the functor \(\Gamma_G(f)\) to be the sheaf associated, for the (fppf) topology (or the (fpqc) topology), to the group subpresheaf of G generated by the image of the morphism of functors f, it is necessary and sufficient that we have: \[ \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} f^n(X^n) = \Gamma_G(f). \] When G is of finite type over k, this condition already implies that there exists an integer n such that \(f^n : X^n \to \Gamma_G(f)\) is covering (fpqc) (and a fortiori surjective).

Set \(X^{\infty} = \coprod_{n\geqslant 1} X^n\); let \(f^{\infty} : X^{\infty} \to \Gamma_G(f)\) be the morphism whose restriction to each of the \(X^n\) is the morphism \(X^n \to \Gamma_G(f)\) deduced from \(f^n\). The sheaf considered in the statement is then the image sheaf of \(X^{\infty}\) under \(f^{\infty}\); hence (IV 4.3), saying that the functor \(\Gamma_G(f)\) is the sheaf in question amounts to saying that \(f^{\infty}\) is covering. Let us show that, for \(f^{\infty}\) to be covering, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f^{\infty}\) be surjective. The condition is evidently necessary; conversely, if \(f^{\infty}\) is surjective, then \(\mu\circ(f^{\infty}\times_k f^{\infty}) : X^{\infty}\times_k X^{\infty} \to \Gamma_G(f)\) is covering (7.5); now, since \(X^n\times_k X^m\) is canonically isomorphic to \(X^{n+m}\), and in this isomorphism \(\mu\circ(f^n\times_k f^m)\) corresponds to \(f^{n+m}\), it is clear that \(\mu\circ(f^{\infty}\times_k f^{\infty})\) factors through \(f^{\infty}\), so that \(f^{\infty}\) is covering. Finally, saying that \(f^{\infty}\) is surjective amounts to saying that \(\bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} f^n(X^n) = \Gamma_G(f)\). If G, hence \(\Gamma_G(f)\), is of finite type, one concludes that there exists n with \(f^n(X^n) = \Gamma_G(f)\), for the \(f^n(X^n)\) are ind-constructible subsets of \(\Gamma_G(f)\) (EGA IV 1.8.4) with union \(\Gamma_G(f)\), and one applies EGA IV 1.9.9. This completes the proof of 7.6.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 7.6.1

Evidently the equivalent conditions of 7.6 imply that the sheaf F in question is representable. The converse is false in general (take X consisting of a single point!). Note, however, that it follows from EGA IV3, 8.14.2 that if F is representable, it is locally of finite presentation over k, so the question is then whether the dominant monomorphism \(F \to \Gamma_G(f)\) is an isomorphism, or again, a closed immersion. This will be the case, by virtue of 1.4.2, if F is of finite type, for example if F is connected (3.5), for example if X is connected and \(f(X)\) contains the unit element of G.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.7

Let k be an algebraically closed field, G a k-group locally of finite type, X a separable k-scheme locally of finite type, \(f : X \to G\) a k-morphism and H a group subscheme of G such that \(H\subset f(X)\). Set \(\Gamma' = \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} f^n(X^n)\). Let \(\Gamma'_0\) (resp. \(H_0\)) be the group of the points of G(k) belonging to \(\Gamma'\) (resp. to H) (cf. 7.2 (viii)). Suppose that \(H_0\) is of finite index in \(\Gamma'_0\). Then there exists an integer m such that \(f^m(X^m) = \Gamma_G(f)\) (cf. 7.6), and \(\Gamma_G(f)\) is a union of a finite number of translates of H.

For every \(n\geqslant 1\), \(f^n(X^n)\) is an ind-constructible subset of G (EGA IV1, 1.9.5 (viii)); hence the same holds for \(\Gamma'\), so that, since G is a Jacobson scheme, \(\Gamma'_0\) is dense in \(\Gamma'\). By hypothesis, there exists a finite sequence \(a_1,\ldots,a_r\) of points of \(\Gamma'_0\) such that \(\Gamma'_0 = a_1 H_0\cup\cdots\cup a_r H_0\), whence \(\Gamma_G(f) = \overline{\Gamma'} = \overline{\Gamma'_0} = \overline{a_1.H_0\cup\ldots\cup a_r.H_0} = \overline{a_1.H_0}\cup\ldots\cup\overline{a_r.H_0} = \overline{a_1.H}\cup\ldots\cup\overline{a_r.H}\), since translation by \(a_i\) is a homeomorphism of G onto G, so that \(\Gamma_G(f) = a_1 H\cup\cdots\cup a_r H\). It is on the other hand clear that there exists an integer p such that each of the \(a_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant r\)) belongs to \(f^p(X^p)\); finally, since \(H\subset f(X)\), for every i we have \(a_i\cdot H\subset f^{p+1}(X^{p+1})\), so that \(f^{p+1}(X^{p+1}) = \Gamma_G(f)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.8

Let G be a k-group locally of finite type, A and B two separable (i.e. smooth) k-subgroup schemes of G. Suppose that one of the following conditions a) or b) is satisfied:

a) A and B are normal and of finite type over k.

b) A is connected and B is of finite type over k.

Then (A,B) is of finite type over k, and the functor defined by (A,B) is the sheaf associated, for the (fppf) topology (or the (fpqc) topology), to the group presheaf of commutators of A and B in G. Moreover, \((A,B)^0 = (A,B^0)\cdot(A^0,B)\).

By 7.6, to show that (A,B) is the desired associated sheaf, it suffices to show that there exists an integer n such that \(\nu^n((A\times_k B)^n) = (A,B)\) (notations of 7.2 (vii)). To show this, as well as to prove the two other assertions, we may assume k algebraically closed (7.1 (iii), EGA IV 2.6.1 and 2.7.1. and VIA 1.1). Let then \(B^0,\ldots,B^p\) be the connected components of B and \(A^0,\ldots,A^q\) those of A (these components are finite in number since A and B are assumed of finite type over k, hence noetherian). Let \(\nu_{ij}\) be the restriction of \(\nu\) to \(A^i\times_k B^j\). Then each of the \(A^i\) and the \(B^j\) is irreducible (VIA 4.1), hence so are \(A^0\times_k B^j\) and \(A^i\times_k B^0\). Since the identity element of G belongs to \(A^0\) and to \(B^0\), it belongs to \(\nu_{0j}(A^0\times_k B^j)\) and to \(\nu_{i0}(A^i\times_k B^0)\). Then each of the \(\Gamma_G(\nu_{0j})\) and the \(\Gamma_G(\nu_{i0})\) is connected (7.2 (vi)). Likewise, if \(u_{0j}\) (resp. \(u_{i0}\)) denotes the injection of \(\Gamma_G(\nu_{0j})\) (resp. \(\Gamma_G(\nu_{i0})\)) into G, \((A^0,B) = \Gamma_G((u_{0j})^p_{j=0})\) and \((A,B^0) = \Gamma_G((u_{i0})^q_{i=0})\) are connected. Moreover, one deduces easily from 7.4 and the preceding constructions that there exists an index r such that \((A^0,B)\) and \((A,B^0)\) are contained in \(\nu^r((A\times_k B)^r)\). In case b), we have \((A,B) = (A^0,B)\), and we are done.

Let us now consider case a). Let H be the group subscheme of G generated by the union of \((A,B^0)\) and \((A^0,B)\); again by 7.4, H is connected, and there exists an integer m such that \(\nu^m((A\times_k B)^m)\supset H\). Hence the set underlying H is none other than \((A,B^0)\cdot(A^0,B)\).

Given a subset X of G stable under the group law (cf. 3.0), we shall denote by \(X_0\) the group of the closed points of G belonging to X. Set \(\Gamma' = \bigcup_{q\geqslant 1} \nu^q((A\times_k B)^q)\). Then, by prop. 7.9 below, we have: \((A^0,B)_0 = (A^0_0,B_0)\), \((A,B^0)_0 = (A_0,B^0_0)\) and \(\Gamma'_0 = (A_0,B_0)\), so that \(H_0 = (A^0_0,B_0)\cdot(A_0,B^0_0)\) is of finite index in \(\Gamma'_0\) (Bible exp. 3, appendix) since \(A_0\) and \(B_0\) are normal, and \(A^0_0\) (resp. \(B^0_0\)) is of finite index in A (resp. B). We are then in the situation of lemma 7.7: since \(\nu^m((A\times_k B)^m)\supset H\), there exists an integer p such that \(\nu^{mp}((A\times_k B)^{mp}) = (A,B)\), and there exists a finite sequence \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) of closed points of G such that \((A,B) = a_1 H\cup\cdots\cup a_n H\). Then, since H is of finite type over k, each of the \(a_i H\) is quasi-compact, hence their union \((A,B)\) is quasi-compact, hence of finite type over k. Since H is irreducible, so is each of the \(a_i H\), and since \(e\in H\), it is clear that \(H = (A,B)^0\), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.9

Let k be an algebraically closed field, G a k-group locally of finite type.

  1. (i)

    Let A and B be two ind-constructible subsets of G. Denote by \(A_0 = A(k)\subset G(k)\) the set of the closed points of G belonging to A. Then \((A\cdot B)_0 = A_0\cdot B_0\), the second product being taken in the group \(G_0 = G(k)\).

  2. (ii)

    Let X be a separable k-scheme locally of finite type, and \(f : X \to G\) a k-morphism. Set \(\Gamma'_G(f) = \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} f^n(X^n)\). Then \(\Gamma'_G(f)_0\) is the subgroup of \(G_0 = G(k)\) generated by \(f(X)_0\).

  3. (iii)

    In particular, let A and B be two smooth group subschemes of G; set \(\Gamma' = \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1} \nu^n(A\times_k B)^n\) (notations of 7.2 (vii)). Then \(\Gamma'_0\) is the group of commutators of \(A_0\) and \(B_0\) in G.

Let us first prove the first assertion. It is clear that \(A_0\cdot B_0\subset (A\cdot B)_0\). Conversely, let \(z\in (A,B)_0\). Then \(\mu^{-1}(z)\) is a closed subset of \(G\times_k G\), and \(A\times B\) (cf. 3.0) is an ind-constructible subset of \(G\times_k G\), so that \(\mu^{-1}(z)\cap(A\times B)\) is a nonempty ind-constructible subset of \(G\times_k G\); it therefore contains a closed point of G (EGA IV 10.1.2) whose projections x and y are closed points of G (EGA IV 10.4.7) such that \(x\in A\), \(y\in B\) and \(x\cdot y = z\), so that \((A\cdot B)_0 = A_0\cdot B_0\).

To prove the second assertion, note that, \(f^n\) being locally of finite type, \(f^n(X^n)\) is an ind-constructible subset of G (EGA IV4, 1.9.5). The first assertion then allows one to show by induction that if we set \(A = f(X)_0\), we have: \(f^n(X^n)_0 = (A\cup A^{-1})^n\), and consequently \((\Gamma'_G(f))_0 = \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1}(f^n(X^n))_0 = \bigcup_{n\geqslant 1}(A\cup A^{-1})^n\), which is the subgroup of \(G_0\) generated by \(A = f(X)_0\).
The third assertion follows from the second and the definitions.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.10

Under the conditions of 7.8, if k is algebraically closed, then \((A,B)(k)\) is the subgroup of commutators of A(k) and B(k) in G(k).
Indeed, it suffices to apply 7.9 (iii), 7.8 and 7.6.
8. Solvable and nilpotent group schemes

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, \(\mathscr{T}\) a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\) (cf. IV §4), G a group presheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\), A and B two group subpresheaves of G. We denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}(A,B)\) the group presheaf of commutators of the presheaves A and B in the presheaf G. If G, A and B are sheaves, we denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(A,B)\) the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}(A,B)\).

If A is normal in G, one easily shows that \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(G,G)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(G,A)\)) is the smallest normal group subsheaf C of G such that the quotient sheaf G/C (resp. A/C) is commutative (resp. central in G/C).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.2

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, \(\mathscr{T}\) a topology on \(\mathscr{C}\), G a group sheaf on \(\mathscr{C}\), n a nonnegative integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    If we set \(K_0 = G\), and for \(p\geqslant 1\), \(K_p = \underline{\mathrm{Comm}}(K_{p-1},K_{p-1})\) (resp. \(K_p = \underline{\mathrm{Comm}}(G,K_{p-1})\)), then \(K_n\) is the unit group presheaf.

  2. (ii)

    If we set \(K'_0 = G\), and for \(p\geqslant 1\), \(K'_p = \underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(K'_{p-1},K'_{p-1})\) (resp. \(K'_p = \underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(G,K'_{p-1})\)), then \(K'_n\) is the unit group sheaf.

  3. (iii)

    There exists a sequence \(H_0 = G, H_1,\ldots,H_n\) of normal subsheaves of G such that, for every i, the quotient sheaf \(H_i/H_{i+1}\) (resp. \(G/H_i\)) is commutative (resp. central in \(G/H_{i+1}\)), and that \(H_n\) is the unit sheaf.

It is clear that \(K_n\subset K'_n\); consequently (ii) implies (i). Let us show that (i) implies (ii). It follows from (IV, Remark 4.4.7) that if we denote by \(\overline{A}\) the sheaf associated to a presheaf A, and if A and B are two group subpresheaves of G, then we have: \(\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}_{\mathscr{T}}(\overline{A},\overline{B})\subset \overline{\underline{\mathrm{Comm}}(A,B)}\). One easily deduces by induction that we have \(K'_p\subset \overline{K_p}\), hence \(K'_n\subset \overline{K_n}\); consequently, if \(K_n\) is the unit presheaf, \(\overline{K_n} = K_n\) is the unit sheaf, hence \(K'_n\) is the unit sheaf.
Finally, conditions (ii) and (iii) are equivalent by remark (8.1).

When these conditions are satisfied, the sheaf G is said to be solvable of class n (resp. nilpotent of class n). When there exists an integer n such that these conditions are satisfied, we say that G is solvable (resp. nilpotent). Note that this condition is independent of \(\mathscr{T}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.3

Let k be a field, S a nonempty k-scheme, \(\Omega\) an algebraically closed extension of k, G a smooth k-group of finite type. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    G is solvable of class n (resp. nilpotent of class n).

  2. (ii)

    \(G\times_k S\) is solvable of class n (resp. nilpotent of class n).

  3. (iii)

    The group \(G(\Omega)\) is solvable of class n (resp. nilpotent of class n).

  4. (iv)

    If we set \(K_0 = G\), and for \(p\geqslant 1\), \(K_p = (K_{p-1},K_{p-1})\) (resp. \(K_p = (G,K_{p-1})\)) (cf. 7.8), then \(K_n\) is the unit k-group.
The equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) follows from proposition 8.2, given that the formation of the group presheaf of commutators commutes with base change (IV 1.3).
The equivalence of conditions (i) and (iv) follows from (7.8).
To show that conditions (iii) and (iv) are equivalent, we may assume that \(k = \Omega\), and then the equivalence of conditions (iii) and (iv) follows from 7.10.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.4

Let G be an S-group of finite presentation such that for every \(s\in S\), \(G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\). Let T be the set of the \(s\in S\) such that \(G_s\) is solvable (resp. nilpotent). Then T is locally constructible in S. If we suppose moreover G flat and separated over S (i.e. when G is smooth of finite presentation and separated over S), then T is closed in S.

It is clear that we may assume S affine with ring A. There exist then, by 10.1 and 10.10 b),(*) a noetherian subring A' of A and an A'-group of finite type G' such that \(G'\otimes_{A'} A\) is isomorphic to G. Moreover (EGA IV 10.8.5 and 11.2.6), if G is flat and separated over S, we may assume G' flat and separated over \(\mathrm{Spec}\,A'\). Likewise (EGA IV, 9.7.7 and 9.3.3), we may assume that, for every \(s'\in S'\), \(G'_{s'}\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s')\). On the other hand, since, if \(s'\) denotes the image of s in \(\mathrm{Spec}\,A'\), we have \(G'_{s'}\otimes_{\kappa(s')}\kappa(s)\simeq G_s\), for \(G_s\) to be solvable (resp. nilpotent), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(G'_{s'}\) (8.3). We are thus reduced to the case where S is an affine noetherian scheme.
(*) In the course of this proof we shall use results established in section 10, which do not depend, any more than section 9 does, on the present section 8.

Let us then show that T is constructible. Applying the criterion (EGA 0III, 9.2.3), one sees, arguing as before, that we are reduced to showing that, in the case where S is noetherian and integral, T or \(S-T\) contains a nonempty open subset of S.

So suppose S integral noetherian with generic point \(\eta\). Set, for every \(s\in S\), \(K^0_s = G_s\), and \(K^p_s = (K^{p-1}_s,K^{p-1}_s)\) (resp. \(K^p_s = (G,K^{p-1}_s)\)). Let us first show that the sequence of closed subschemes \(K^p_\eta\) is stationary; it follows from (7.3 (v)) that each of the \(K^p_\eta\) is normal, hence the sequence of the \(K^p_\eta\) is decreasing; this sequence is then stationary since \(G_\eta\) is noetherian; there exists therefore an integer n such that, for every \(p\geqslant n\), we have: \(K^p_\eta = K^n_\eta\).

On the other hand, we shall see in 10.12 and 10.13 that there exist a nonempty open subset S' of S and an S'-group of finite presentation D such that for every \(s\in S'\), we have: \(D_s = K^n_s\) and \((D_s,D_s) = D_s\) (resp. \((G_s,D_s) = D_s\)). We may assume that \(S' = S\). Then, for every \(s\in S\) and every \(p\geqslant n\), we have \(D_s = K^p_s\), so that \(G_s\) is solvable (resp. nilpotent) if and only if \(D_s\) is isomorphic to the unit \(\kappa(s)\)-group.

But by EGA IV3, 9.6.1 (xi), the set of the \(s\in S'\) such that the structural morphism \(D_s \to \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s)\) is an isomorphism is constructible, hence either is nowhere dense, or contains a nonempty open subset of S.

Let us show that if G is flat and separated over S, T is closed; since T is locally constructible, for T to be closed (EGA IV 1.10.1), it is necessary and sufficient that T be stable under specialization.

So let \(s\in S\) and \(s'\) a specialization of s in S. Since we have reduced to the case where S is noetherian, by EGA II, 7.1.9, there exist a discrete valuation ring A and a morphism \(\mathrm{Spec}\,A\to S\) such that s (resp. s') is the image of the generic point \(\alpha\) (resp. of the closed point a) of \(\mathrm{Spec}\,A\). It then suffices to show that if we set \(G' = G\otimes_S A\), and if \(G'_\alpha\) is solvable (resp. nilpotent), the same holds for \(G'_a\). Note that, since G is flat and separated over S, \(G'\) is flat and separated over A, so that we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A.

Since \(G'_\alpha\) is assumed solvable (resp. nilpotent), there exists an integer q such that \(K^q_\alpha\) (with the notations introduced above) is isomorphic to the unit \(\kappa(\alpha)\)-group. Let \(\overline{K^n}\) be the schematic closure (in the sense of EGA 2.8.5) of \(K^n_\alpha\) in G. Let us show, by induction on p, that \((\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a \supset K^p_a\). First note that, since G is flat over A, \(\overline{G}_\alpha\) is equal to G (EGA IV2, 2.8.5), hence \((K^0_\alpha)_a = K^0_a\).

Denote by \(\overline{\nu},\overline{\nu}_a,\nu_a,\nu_\alpha\) the morphisms: \[ \overline{K^p}\underset{A}{\times}\overline{K^p} \longrightarrow G, \quad (\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}(\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a \longrightarrow G_a, \quad K^p_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}K^p_a \longrightarrow G_a, \quad K^p_\alpha\underset{\kappa(\alpha)}{\times}K^p_\alpha \longrightarrow G_\alpha \] resp. \[ G\underset{A}{\times}\overline{K^p} \longrightarrow G, \quad G_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}(\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a \longrightarrow G_a, \quad G_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}K^p_a \longrightarrow G_a, \quad G_\alpha\underset{\kappa(\alpha)}{\times}K^p_\alpha \longrightarrow G_\alpha, \] defined as in 7.2 (vii). Then, since \(\nu_\alpha\) factors through \(K^{p+1}_\alpha\), \(\overline{\nu}\) factors through \(\overline{K^{p+1}}\), which is evidently a group subscheme of G, hence \(\overline{K^{p+1}}\) contains \(\Gamma_G(\overline{\nu})\). By 7.1 (iii), \(\Gamma_G(\overline{\nu})_a = \Gamma_{G_a}(\overline{\nu}_a)\); by the induction hypothesis, \[ K^p_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}K^p_a \subset (\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}(\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a \qquad \text{resp.} \qquad G_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}K^p_a \subset G_a\underset{\kappa(a)}{\times}(\overline{K^p_\alpha})_a, \] so that \(K^{p+1}_a = \Gamma_{G_a}(\nu_a) \subset \Gamma_{G_a}(\overline{\nu}_a) \subset (\overline{K^{p+1}_\alpha})_a\).

But since \(K^q_\alpha\) is isomorphic to the unit \(\kappa(\alpha)\)-group, and the unit A-group is flat over A and is isomorphic to a closed subscheme of G (since G is separated over A, cf. 5.1), it follows from EGA IV2, 2.8.5 that \(\overline{K^q_\alpha}\) is isomorphic to the unit A-group, hence that \(K^q_a\subset(\overline{K^q_\alpha})_a\) is isomorphic to the unit \(\kappa(a)\)-group, so that \(G_a\) is solvable (resp. nilpotent), q.e.d.
9. Quotient sheaves

The present section essentially confines itself to a review, in the special case of homogeneous spaces and groups, of well-known general facts on passage to the quotient by flat equivalence relations (cf. Exp. IV).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 9.1

Given a monomorphism \(u : G' \to G\) of S-groups, we denote by \(G/G'\) (resp. \(G'\backslash G\)), and call the right (resp. left) quotient sheaf of G by G', the sheaf (for the (fpqc) topology) quotient of G by the equivalence relation defined by the monomorphism \(G\times_S G' \xrightarrow{\ \delta\circ(\mathrm{id}_G\times u)\ } G\times_S G\) (resp. \(G'\times_S G \xrightarrow{\ \gamma\circ(u\times\mathrm{id}_G)\ } G\times_S G\)), where \(\delta\) (resp. \(\gamma\)) denotes the automorphism of \(G\times_S G\) defined by \((g,h)\mapsto (g,gh)\) (resp. \((h,g)\mapsto (hg,g)\)) for \(g\in G(T)\) and \(h\in G'(T)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.2

Let \(u : G' \to G\) be a monomorphism of S-groups. Suppose that G/G' is representable by an S-scheme G''. Then:

  1. (i)

    The canonical morphism \(p : G \to G''\) is covering for the (fpqc) topology.

  2. (ii)

    If we set \(\varepsilon'' = p\circ\varepsilon\) (this morphism is called the unit section of G''), the following diagrams are cartesian: \[ \xymatrix{ G\times_S G' \ar[r]^-{\mu\circ(\mathrm{id}_G\times u)} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & G \ar[d]^-{p} \\ G \ar[r]_-{p} & G'' } \qquad,\qquad \xymatrix{ G' \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\pi'} & G \ar[d]^-{p} \\ S \ar[r]_-{\varepsilon''} & G'' } \ . \] In particular, u is an immersion.

  3. (iii)

    There exists on G'' a unique structure of S-scheme with left operator group G, such that p is a morphism of S-schemes with operator group G.

  4. (iv)

    If we suppose moreover that G' is normal in G, there exists on G'' a unique structure of S-group such that p is a morphism of S-groups.

  5. (v)

    Let \(S_0\) be an S-scheme; set \(G_0 = G\times_S S_0\) and \(G'_0 = G'\times_S S_0\); then \(G_0/G'_0\) is representable by \(G''_0 = G''\times_S S_0\).

  6. (vi)

    Let \(\mathscr{P}\) be a property for an S-morphism. Suppose \(\mathscr{P}\) stable under base change; then if p satisfies \(\mathscr{P}\), so does the structural morphism \(\pi' : G' \to S\).

  7. (vii)

    Let \(\mathscr{P}\) be a property for an S-morphism. Suppose \(\mathscr{P}\) of a local nature for the (fpqc) topology (cf. 2.0 and 2.1.2). Then, for the morphism \(p : G \to G''\) to satisfy \(\mathscr{P}\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(\pi' : G' \to S\).

  8. (viii)

    Let \(\mathscr{P}\) be a property for an S-morphism; suppose \(\mathscr{P}\) of a local nature for the (fpqc) topology, and stable under composition; then, if the structural morphisms \(G''\to S\) and \(G'\to S\) satisfy \(\mathscr{P}\), so does the structural morphism \(G\to S\).

  9. (ix)

    Suppose G reduced; then G'' is reduced.

  10. (x)

    For G'' to be separated over S, it is necessary and sufficient that u be a closed immersion, or again that \(\varepsilon''\) be a closed immersion.

  11. (xi)

    For G' to be flat over S, it is necessary and sufficient that p be a flat morphism (or, what amounts to the same thing, faithfully flat).

    In this case, for G'' to be flat over S, it is necessary and sufficient that G be flat over S.

  12. (xii)

    For G' to be flat and locally of finite presentation over S, it is necessary and sufficient that \(p : G \to G''\) be faithfully flat and locally of finite presentation.

    Under these conditions, for G'' to be locally of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite type, of finite type, smooth, étale, unramified, locally quasi-finite, quasi-finite) over S, it suffices that the same hold for G over S (and the condition is also necessary in the first two cases, cf. (viii)).

1. (xiii)

    Suppose G' flat and of finite presentation over S.

    a) Then p is of finite presentation and faithfully flat;

    b) moreover, for G to be of finite presentation over S, it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for G''.

Recall that the equivalence relation under consideration is universally effective (IV 4.9). Then assertions (i), (iii), (iv), (v) and the first assertion of (ii) follow from IV 4.3, 2.2, 2.4, 4.5 and 3.2 (iii). The second assertion of (ii) follows from the first, as the following cartesian diagram shows, since \((G\times_S G')\times_G S\) is isomorphic to G': \[ \xymatrix{ G' \ar[r]^-{((\varepsilon\circ\pi'),\,\mathrm{id}_{G'})} \ar[d]_-{\pi'} & G\times_S G' \ar[r]^-{\mu\circ(\mathrm{id}_G\times u)} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & G \ar[d]^-{p} \\ S \ar[r]_-{\varepsilon} & G \ar[r]_-{p} & G'' } \]

Finally, it is clear that \(\varepsilon''\) is an S-section of G'', hence an immersion (EGA I, 5.3.13); by the preceding cartesian diagram, the same holds for u, which completes the proof of (ii). Moreover, (vi) is an immediate consequence of the second cartesian diagram of (ii).

Let us prove (vii). By (i), p is covering for the (fpqc) topology; hence, by (ii), to show that p satisfies \(\mathscr{P}\), it suffices to show that the first projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1 : G\times_S G' \to G\) satisfies \(\mathscr{P}\), which follows from the fact that \(\mathscr{P}\) is stable under base change, since \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) comes from \(\pi'\) by base change.
It is clear that (viii) follows from (vii), for \(\pi = \pi''\circ p\), where \(\pi'' : G'' \to S\) denotes the structural morphism.

Let us prove (ix). By (i), p is an epimorphism; since G is reduced, p factors through the immersion \(G''_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \to G\), which is therefore also an epimorphism, hence an isomorphism (IV 4.4).

Let us prove (x). If G'' is separated over S, \(\varepsilon''\) is a closed immersion (EGA I 5.4.6). By (ii), if \(\varepsilon''\) is a closed immersion, the same holds for u; finally, if u is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(\delta\circ(\mathrm{id}_G\times u) : G\times_S G' \to G\times_S G\); hence, by lemma (9.2.1) below, G'' is separated over S.
Assertion (xi) follows from (vii) and EGA IV2, 2.2.13.

Assertion (xii) follows from (vii), from EGA IV4, 17.7.5 and 17.7.7, and from the fact that, p being universally open, for every \(s\in S\), if the space underlying \(G_s\) is discrete, the same holds for the space underlying \(G''_s\).
Finally, assertion (xiii) follows from (vii), (viii), and EGA IV4, 17.7.5.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.2.1

Let X be an S-scheme and R an equivalence relation defined on X by the monomorphism \(v : R \to X\times_S X\). Suppose R effective. Then:

  1. (i)

    v is an immersion.

  2. (ii)

    For \(Y = X/R\) to be separated over S, it is necessary and sufficient that v be a closed immersion.

Recall (IV 3.2) that by definition the natural morphism \(R \to X\times_Y X\) is an isomorphism. One deduces from it (EGA I, 5.3.5) the following cartesian diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{v} \ar[d] & X\times_S X \ar[d] \\ Y \ar[r]_-{\Delta_{Y/S}} & Y\times_S Y } \]

Then, since \(\Delta_{Y/S}\) is an immersion (EGA I, 5.3.9), the same holds for v. Likewise, if Y is separated over S, \(\Delta_{Y/S}\) is a closed immersion, hence so is v. Conversely, since p is covering for the (fpqc) topology (IV 4.3), since the property for a morphism of being a closed immersion is of a local nature for the (fpqc) topology (EGA IV2, 2.7.1), and since \(p\times_S p\) is also covering for the (fpqc) topology (IV 2.3), if v is a closed immersion, the same holds for \(\Delta_{Y/S}\), hence Y is separated over S.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9.2.2

Under the general hypotheses of 9.2, if we suppose G' flat and locally of finite presentation over S, then p is covering for the (fpqc) topology (9.2 (vii)), hence assertions (vii) and (viii) of 9.2 can be extended to properties \(\mathscr{P}\) of a local nature for the (fppf) topology.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9.3

a) The question of whether or not a quotient G/G' is representable is often delicate; in this seminar we prove the representability of certain particular quotients.

In general, in order to be able to assert that the quotient G/G' is representable, it does not suffice to assume G and G' of finite presentation over S and G' flat over S, for even when G and G' are smooth over S, it may happen that G has connected fibers and that G' is not closed in G; consequently G/G' would have to be separated (5.3, which is in contradiction with 9.2 (x)). To obtain such a counterexample, one can take for S the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and set \(G = \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\). Consider on the other hand an integer \(n>1\), invertible on S; then \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n = \mathrm{Ker}(G \xrightarrow{\ n\ } G)\) is a closed subgroup of G, étale over S (cf. VIIA). Let G' be the open subgroup of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) obtained by removing from \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) the closed subset of the closed fiber of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) complementary to the origin. Then G' is not closed in G, hence G/G' is not representable. (One can also construct such examples where G' is smooth with connected fibers.)

b) On the other hand, it is not ruled out that G/G' is representable when G and G' are of finite presentation over S, and G' is flat over S and closed in G.(*) Under these hypotheses, it is known that G/G' is representable in the following special cases:

1° – S is the spectrum of an artinian ring (cf. VIA 2).

2° – G' is proper over S and G quasi-projective over S (cf. V 1).

3° – S is regular of dimension 1 (Raynaud, to appear).
(*) This is too optimistic, as shown by M. Raynaud in his thesis (loc. cit. X 14).

pages.title: 10. Passage to the projective limit in group schemes and schemes with operator group

blocks.html: 10. Passage to the projective limit in group schemes and schemes with operator group

Recall the essential result of EGA IV3, §8.8: Given the following situation: \(S_0\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, I an increasing filtered preordered set, \((\mathscr{A}_i)_{i\in I}\) an inductive system of commutative quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-Algebras, \(\mathscr{A} = \varinjlim \mathscr{A}_i\), \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}_i\) for \(i\in I\), and \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}\), then the category of S-schemes of finite presentation is determined up to equivalence by the data of the categories of \(S_i\)-schemes of finite presentation, of the functors between these categories \(\rho_{ij} : X_i \mapsto X_i\times_{S_i} S_j\) for \(i\leqslant j\), and transitivity isomorphisms \(\rho_{jk}\circ\rho_{ij} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \rho_{ik}\). Let us be precise. Given \(j\in I\) and an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation \(X_j\), we set, for every \(i\in I\) such that \(i\geqslant j\), \(X_i = X_j\times_{S_j} S_i\), and \(X = X_j\times_{S_j} S\). Then (EGA IV3, 8.8.2):

1. (i)

    Given \(j\in I\) and two \(S_j\)-schemes of finite presentation \(X_j\) and \(Y_j\), the canonical map from \(\varinjlim_{i\geqslant j}\mathrm{Hom}_{S_i}(X_i,Y_i)\) to \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) is bijective.

  2. (ii)

    For every S-scheme of finite presentation X, there exist an index \(j\in I\), an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation \(X_j\) and an S-isomorphism \(X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X_j\times_{S_j} S\).

One concludes from this (EGA IV3, 8.8.3) that, whenever we have a diagram D involving a finite number of objects and arrows of the category of S-schemes of finite presentation, one can find an index \(i\in I\) and a diagram \(D_i\) in the category of \(S_i\)-schemes of finite presentation, such that the diagram D comes, up to isomorphism, from the diagram \(D_i\) by the base change \(S\to S_i\). One can even find i and \(D_i\) such that every cartesian square of D comes from a cartesian square of \(D_i\).

Moreover, a great number of common properties for a morphism, stable under base change, have the following property: given an index \(j\in I\), two \(S_j\)-schemes of finite presentation \(X_j\) and \(Y_j\) and an \(S_j\)-morphism \(u_j : X_j \to Y_j\), for \(u = u_j\times_{S_j} S\) to have the property \(\mathscr{P}\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an index \(i\in I\) such that \(j\leqslant i\) and that \(u_i = u_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) have the property \(\mathscr{P}\). This is the case when \(\mathscr{P}\) is one of the following properties for a morphism: being separated, surjective, radicial, affine, quasi-affine, finite, quasi-finite, proper, projective, quasi-projective, an isomorphism, a monomorphism, an immersion, an open immersion, a closed immersion, smooth, unramified or étale (EGA IV 8.10.5, 11.2.6 and 17.7.6).

Note that this is also the case when \(\mathscr{P}\) is the property of being covering for the faithfully flat locally of finite presentation topology (denoted fppf); indeed, given two S-schemes of finite presentation X and Y, and an S-morphism \(u : X \to Y\), it follows from IV 3.1 (i) that, for u to be covering for the (fppf) topology, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an S-scheme Z and an S-morphism \(v : Z \to Y\), faithfully flat and of finite presentation, that factors through u.

The aim of this section is to give variants of this kind of result for the category of S-groups of finite presentation, that of S-schemes with operator group, and for certain properties of group monomorphisms (being normal, central with representable quotient sheaf, etc.).
The two preliminary results of this type are the following:

In the situation recalled at the beginning of (10.0), let \(j\in I\), and let \(G_j\) and \(H_j\) be two \(S_j\)-groups of finite presentation; set, for every \(i\in I\) such that \(j\leqslant i\), \(G_i = G_j\times_{S_j} S_i\), \(G = G_j\times_{S_j} S\), and define likewise \(H_i\) and H. Then the canonical map from \(\varinjlim_{i\geqslant j}\mathrm{Hom}_{S_i\text{-gr.}}(G_i,H_i)\) to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H)\) is bijective.

In the situation recalled at the beginning of (10.0), let G be an S-group of finite presentation; then there exist an index \(j\in I\), an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation \(G_j\), and an S-isomorphism of groups from G onto \(G_j\times_{S_j} S\).
Assertions 10.2 and 10.3 are easy consequences of 10.0 and 10.1, taking into account the interpretation of the S-group structure given in EGA 0III, 8.2.5 and 8.2.6.

Let \(j\in I\), let \(G_j\) and \(H_j\) be two \(S_j\)-groups of finite presentation, and \(u_j : G_j \to H_j\) a morphism of \(S_j\)-groups. For \(u = u_j\times_{S_j} S\) to be a central monomorphism (resp. a normal monomorphism), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that \(u_i = u_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) has the same property.
This is an immediate consequence of 10.0 and 10.1, taking into account the characterization given in 6.7 of central and normal group monomorphisms.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10.5

Let \(j\in I\), and let \(G_j\) be an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation. For \(G = G_j\times_{S_j} S\) to be commutative, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that \(G_i = G_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) is commutative.
Indeed, saying that an S-group is commutative amounts to the same thing as saying that, considered as a group subscheme of itself, it is central.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 10.6

In the situation recalled at the beginning of 10.0, let \(j\in I\), \(G_j\) an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation, \(G'_j\) a group subscheme of \(G_j\), flat and of finite presentation over \(S_j\). For \((G_j\times_{S_j} S)/(G'_j\times_{S_j} S)\) to be representable for the (fpqc) topology, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that \((G_j\times_{S_j} S_i)/(G'_j\times_{S_j} S_i)\) is representable.
This is a consequence of the following more general lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 10.7

Let \(\mathscr{T}\) be the (fppf) or (fpqc) topology; let \(j\in I\), \(X_j\) an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation, and \(R_j\) an equivalence relation on \(X_j\) defined by an \(S_j\)-monomorphism \(v_j : R_j \to X_j\times_{S_j} X_j\) such that the morphism \(\mathrm{pr}_{1,j}\circ v_j : R_j \to X_j\) deduced from \(v_j\) by composition with the first projection \(X_j\times_{S_j} X_j \to X_j\) is flat and of finite presentation. Then, for the quotient sheaf \((X_j\times_{S_j} S)/(R_j\times_{S_j} S)\) for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that the quotient sheaf \((X_j\times_{S_j} S_i)/(R_j\times_{S_j} S_i)\) for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable.
Taking into account the statements of EGA IV2, 8.8.2, 8.8.3, 8.10.5 and 11.2.6 recalled in 10.0, this lemma is a consequence of the following result:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 10.8

Let \(\mathscr{T}\) be the (fppf) or (fpqc) topology; let X be an S-scheme of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite presentation), R an equivalence relation on X defined by a monomorphism \(v : R \to X\times_S X\) such that \(\mathrm{pr}_1\circ v\) is flat and of finite presentation (resp. flat and locally of finite presentation). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The quotient sheaf X/R for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable.

  2. (ii)

    There exist an S-scheme of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite presentation) Y and a faithfully flat morphism \(p : X \to Y\) such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1\circ v} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2\circ v} & X \ar[d]^-{p} \\ X \ar[r]_-{p} & Y } \] (where \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) are the projections \(X\times_S X \rightrightarrows X\)) is cartesian.

First note that by IV 3.2 and 4.3, for the sheaf X/R for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) to be representable by Y, it is necessary and sufficient that the diagram (D) be cartesian and that p be covering for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\).

Let us show that (i) implies (ii). Hypothesis (i) implies that the diagram (D) is cartesian, hence that \(\mathrm{pr}_1\circ v\) is deduced from p by base change by p, and that p is covering for the (fpqc) topology. Since \(\mathrm{pr}_1\circ v\) is faithfully flat and of finite presentation (resp. faithfully flat and locally of finite presentation), the same holds for p (EGA IV2, 2.7.1), and as X is of finite presentation (resp. locally of finite presentation) over S, the same holds for Y (EGA IV 17.7.4).

Let us show that (ii) implies (i). It suffices to show that p is covering for the fppf topology; now p is faithfully flat by hypothesis, and is locally of finite presentation since X and Y are locally of finite presentation over S.

In the situation recalled at the beginning of 10.0, let \(j\in I\), and let \(G_j\) be an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation. For \(G^0 = (G_j\times_{S_j} S)^0\) to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that \((G_i)^0 = (G_j\times_{S_j} S_i)^0\) is representable.
The condition is sufficient (3.3).

Let us show that the condition is necessary. Indeed, \(G^0\) is of finite presentation over S and open in G (3.9). By (10.0), there exist \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), and an open group subscheme \(G^0_i\) of \(G_i\) such that \(G^0_i\times_{S_i} S = G^0\). The structural morphism \(G^0 \to S\) is connected (i.e. with geometrically connected fibers (VIA 1.1)). Then (EGA IV 9.3.3 and 9.7.7), after increasing i if necessary, we may assume that the structural morphism \(G^0_i \to S\) is connected, hence that the space underlying \(G^0_i\) is none other than \(\underline{(G_i)^0}\) (3.10.1), hence that \(G^0_i\) represents \((G_i)^0\).
Let us recall two very useful special cases of the situation stated in 10.0:

a) Given a point x of a scheme X, one sets \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}\) and considers the decreasing filtered family \((S_i)_{i\in I}\) of affine open neighborhoods of x; then \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\). In particular, if x is the generic point of an integral scheme X, one gets \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(x)\).

b) One sets \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}\), and considers the family \((\mathscr{A}_i)_{i\in I}\), preordered by inclusion, of the finite type sub-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras of the ring of an affine scheme S. Given that the \(\mathscr{A}_i\) are noetherian rings, this allows one in many cases to pass from the noetherian case to the general case.
We shall now give two results concerning the special case envisaged in a).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 10.11

Let S be an integral scheme with generic point \(\eta\), G an S-group of finite presentation, X an S-scheme of finite presentation, u and v two S-morphisms from X to G, \(i : H \to G\) and \(j : K \to G\) two monomorphisms of finite presentation. Then:

  1. (i)

    there exists a nonempty open subset S' of S such that if we denote by G' (resp. H', resp. X', resp. u', resp. v') the restriction of G (resp. H, resp. X, resp. u, resp. v) above the open subset S' of S, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(u',v')\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(u')\), resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{G'} H'\)) is representable by a closed sub-S'-scheme of finite presentation \(T'_1\) (resp. \(C'_1\), resp. \(C'_2\)) of G'.

  2. (ii)

    suppose that \(K_\eta\) is closed in \(G_\eta\) (which is the case if K is a sub-S-group scheme of G (1.4.2)); then there exists a nonempty open subset S' of S such that if we denote by G' (resp. H', resp. K') the restriction of G (resp. H, resp. K) above the open subset S' of S, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{G'}(H',K')\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{G'} K'\)) is representable by a closed sub-S'-scheme of finite presentation \(T'_2\) (resp. \(N'\)) of G'.

  3. (iii)

    suppose that \(H_\eta\) and \(K_\eta\) are closed in \(G_\eta\) (which is the case if H and K are group subschemes of G (1.4.2)); then there exists a nonempty open subset S' of S such that if we denote by G' (resp. H', resp. K') the restriction of G (resp. H, resp. K) above the open subset S' of S, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_{G'}(H',K')\) is representable by a closed sub-S'-scheme of finite presentation of G'.

Since \(G_\eta\), \(X_\eta\), \(H_\eta\), \(K_\eta\) are flat over the field \(\kappa(\eta)\), and \(G_\eta\) is separated over \(\kappa(\eta)\) (VIA 2), by (10.2, 10.10 a), EGA IV 8.8.2 and 8.10.5), there exists an affine open subset S' of S such that G' is an S'-group of finite presentation, flat and separated over S', and X', H' and K' are flat and of finite presentation over S'. If we suppose \(H_\eta\) (resp. \(K_\eta\)) closed in \(G_\eta\), one can choose S' so that H' (resp. K') is closed in G'. By (EGA IV 11.3.15), after replacing S' by an affine open subset of S' if necessary, we may assume that G', H', K' and X' are essentially free over S' (in the sense of VIII (6.1)). It then follows from (VIII 5.5 b) and e))(*) that, under the hypotheses of the statement, the functors under consideration are representable by closed subschemes (hence of finite presentation over S') of G'.
(*) See the note (*) in the proof of proposition 6.2.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 10.12

Let S be an integral scheme, G an S-group of finite presentation, A and B two group subschemes of G of finite presentation, with smooth generic fiber, such that A has connected generic fiber (resp. A and B are normal in G). Then there exist a nonempty open subset S' of S and a closed group subscheme D', of finite presentation, with smooth fibers, of \(G' = G|S'\), such that D' has connected fibers (resp. is normal in G'), and such that D' represents the sheaf associated, for the (fppf) or (fpqc) topology, to the group presheaf of commutators of A and B in G. In particular, for every \(s\in S'\), we have \(D'_s = (A_s,B_s)\) with the notations of 7.2 (vii).

Let \(\eta\) be the generic point of S; set \(D'_\eta = (A_\eta,B_\eta)\). By 7.8 (resp. 7.3 v)), \(D'_\eta\) is connected (resp. normal in \(G_\eta\)). By 10.1 and 10.10 a), there exist a nonempty open subset S' of S and a sub-S'-group scheme D' of finite presentation and closed in G', with separable fibers (EGA IV 9.7.7 and 9.3.3), having \(D'_\eta\) as fiber at the point \(\eta\), and such that D' has connected fibers (i.e. geometrically connected (VIA 1.1)) (resp. is normal in G') (EGA IV 9.7.7 and 9.3.3 (resp. 10.4)). Moreover, we saw, in the course of the proof of (7.8), that there exists an integer n such that \(\nu^n_\eta((A_\eta\times_{\kappa(\eta)} B_\eta)^n) = D'\), where \(\nu_\eta : A_\eta\times_{\kappa(\eta)} B_\eta \to G_\eta\) is defined as in (7.2 (vii)). We can define, by the same formulas as in (7.2 (vii)) and 7.1 (ii), the morphisms \(\nu' : A'\times_{S'} B' \to G'\) and \(\nu'^n : (A'\times_{S'} B')^n \to G'\), and indeed \(\nu'^n_{(\kappa(\eta))} = \nu^n_\eta\). Consequently (EGA IV 8.8.3, 8.10.5 and 11.2.6), one can choose S' such that the morphism \(\nu'^n\) is flat and factors through D', and such that the morphism \((A'\times_{S'} B')^n \to D'\) deduced from \(\nu'^n\) is surjective. Then (7.5) the morphism \((A'\times_{S'} B')^{n+1} \to D'\) deduced from \(\nu'^{n+1}\) is covering for the fppf topology (and a fortiori for the fpqc topology). Hence D' represents the sheaf associated, for the fppf or fpqc topology, to the presheaf of commutators of A and B in G. Moreover, for every \(s\in S'\), the morphism \((A_s\times_{\kappa(s)} B_s)^n \to D'_s\) deduced from \(\nu^n_s\) is surjective; hence (7.6), \((A_s,B_s)\) represents the sheaf of commutators of \(A_s\) and \(B_s\) in \(G_s\) (for the fppf or fpqc topology), whence \(D'_s = (A_s,B_s)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10.13

Let S be an integral scheme with generic point \(\eta\), G an S-group of finite presentation, D a sub-S-group scheme of G of finite presentation, with smooth fibers and normal in G. Suppose that \((D_\eta,D_\eta) = D_\eta\) (resp. \((G_\eta,D_\eta) = D_\eta\)). Then there exists a nonempty open subset S' of S such that for every \(s\in S'\), we have \((D_s,D_s) = D_s\) (resp. \((G_s,D_s) = D_s\)).
This follows immediately from 10.12 and EGA IV3, 8.8.2.5.

The statements 10.2 and 10.3 concerning the category of S-groups of finite presentation extend to the category of pairs consisting of an S-group of finite presentation and an S-scheme of finite presentation with operator group G. Precisely:

(i) In the situation recalled at the beginning of (10.0), let \(j\in I\) and let \(G_j\) and \(G'_j\) be two \(S_j\)-groups of finite presentation, \(H_j\) (resp. \(H'_j\)) an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation with operator group \(G_j\) (resp. \(G'_j\)). Set, for \(i\in I\), \(i> j\), \(G_i = G_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) and \(G = G_j\times_{S_j} S\), and define likewise \(G'_i\), G', \(H_i\), H, \(H'_i\) and H'. Denote by \(\mathrm{Dihom}_{S\text{-gr.}}((G,H),(G',H'))\) the set of di-morphisms of S-groups and of S-schemes with operator group from the pair (G,H) to the pair (G',H'). Then the canonical map from \(\varinjlim_{i\geqslant j}\mathrm{Dihom}_{S_i\text{-gr.}}((G_i,H_i),(G'_i,H'_i))\) to \(\mathrm{Dihom}_{S\text{-gr.}}((G,H),(G',H'))\) is bijective.

(ii) In the situation recalled at the beginning of (10.0), let G be an S-group of finite presentation and H an S-scheme of finite presentation with operator group G; then there exist an index \(j\in J\), an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation \(G_j\), an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation \(H_j\) with operator group \(G_j\) and a di-isomorphism of S-groups and of S-schemes with operator groups from \((G_j\times_{S_j} S, H_j\times_{S_j} S)\) onto (G,H).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 10.15

Given an S-group functor G and an S-functor H with operator group G, we say that H is an S-functor in homogeneous spaces under G for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) if the canonical morphism \(G\times_S H \to H\times_S H\) (defined by \((g,h)\mapsto (g\cdot h,h)\) for \(g\in G(T), h\in H(T)\)) is an epimorphism of sheaves for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\).

If G is an S-group and H is an S-scheme with operator group G, saying that H is an S-scheme in homogeneous spaces under G for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) thus amounts to saying that the canonical morphism \(G\times_S H \to H\times_S H\), defined as above, is covering for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) (IV 4.3).

Likewise, saying that an S-scheme H with operator group an S-group G is a principal homogeneous bundle under G for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\) (IV 1.5) amounts to saying (IV 5.1.6 (ii)) that the canonical morphism \(G\times_S H \to H\times_S H\) is an isomorphism and that the structural morphism \(H\to S\) is covering for the topology \(\mathscr{T}\).

In the situation recalled at the beginning of 10.0, let \(j\in I\), \(G_j\) an \(S_j\)-group of finite presentation, \(H_j\) an \(S_j\)-scheme of finite presentation with operator group \(G_j\). For \(H = H_j\times_{S_j} S\) to be an S-scheme in homogeneous spaces under \(G = G_j\times_{S_j} S\) (resp. a principal homogeneous bundle under G) for the faithfully flat locally of finite presentation topology, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an index \(i\in I\), \(i\geqslant j\), such that \(H_i = H_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) is an \(S_i\)-scheme in homogeneous spaces under \(G_i = G_j\times_{S_j} S_i\) (resp. a principal homogeneous bundle under \(G_i\)).
Taking into account (10.14) and (EGA IV 8.8.2, 8.8.3 and 8.10.5), the statement follows from the property concerning morphisms covering for the fppf topology recalled in (10.0).
11. Affine group schemes
11.0. Notation.
Let S be a scheme, X an S-scheme, \(f : X \to S\) the structural morphism; we set \(\mathscr{A}(X) = f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11.1

Let X and Y be two S-schemes quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, \(f : X \to S\) and \(g : Y \to S\) the structural morphisms. Then the canonical homomorphism \[ \varphi : \mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}(Y) \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}\Bigl(X\underset{S}{\times} Y\Bigr) \] is an isomorphism in each of the following cases:

a) f and g are affine,

b) g (or f) is flat and affine,

c) g is flat and \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) is a flat \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module.

In case b), we may assume that it is g that is flat and affine. Set \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}(X)\), \(Y' = Y\times_S S'\), \(g' = g\times_S S'\) and denote by v the morphism \(S'\to S\). Then Y is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, and either S' is flat over S (case c), by (EGA III (1.4.15.5)), or g is affine (cases a) and b)). Then (EGA III 1.4.15 and IV 1.2.7 or II 1.5.2), we have: \(g'_*(\mathscr{O}_{Y'}) = v^*g_*(\mathscr{O}_Y) = \mathscr{A}(Y)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S'}\).

By (EGA II 1.2.7), f factors through v by means of a morphism \(p : G \to S'\), and we have: \(X\times_S Y = X\times_{S'} Y'\) and \(p_*(\mathscr{O}_X) = \mathscr{O}_{S'}\). Since v and f are quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the same holds for p (EGA IV 1.2.2 and 1.2.4). Either Y is flat over S (cases b) and c)) and then Y' is flat over S', or X is affine over S (case a)), and then p is an isomorphism. In the first case, one can again apply (EGA III 1.4.15 and IV 1.2.7), and in both cases one finds: \(p'_*(\mathscr{O}_{X\times_S Y}) = g'^*p_*(\mathscr{O}_X) = g'^*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathscr{O}_{Y'}\), where \(p' = p\times_{S'} Y'\).

Whence: \(\mathscr{A}(X\times_S Y) = v_*g'_*p'_*(\mathscr{O}_{X\times_S Y}) = v_*g'_*(\mathscr{O}_{Y'}) = v_*(\mathscr{A}(Y)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathscr{A}(Y)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(X)\) by (EGA II 1.4.7).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11.2

One defines a functor \(X\mapsto \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}(X)\) from the full subcategory of \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) of the X-schemes, flat, quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, such that \(\mathscr{A}(X)\) is a flat \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module, to that of the S-schemes flat and affine over S. This functor commutes with finite products, hence transforms S-groups into S-groups.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 11.3

Given an S-group G, flat, quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, such that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), we shall denote by \(G_{\mathrm{af}}\), and call the affine envelope of G, the S-group \(G_{\mathrm{af}} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{A}(G)\).

Let \(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{F}\) be two quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules; denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) the S-functor of morphisms of S-functors from \(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})\) to \(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})\), and by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}), \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) the S-subfunctor of \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) consisting of the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-homomorphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module functors (cf. I). Then \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))(T)\) is in canonical one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{E}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T, \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T)\), i.e. we have \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{W}(\mathscr{E}),\mathbb{W}(\mathscr{F}))\).

Let X be an S-scheme. Then the set of morphisms of S-functors from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(X\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\), hence the set of morphisms of S-functors from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) is in one-to-one correspondence with a subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(X\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{A}(X\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})))\) (EGA II 1.7.13).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11.5

Let X be an S-scheme quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, \(f : X \to S\) the structural morphism, \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) two quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules. We suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

a) f is affine

b) \(\mathscr{F}\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_S\).

Then:

(i) the canonical homomorphism \(\varphi : \mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathscr{A}(X\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}))\) is an isomorphism (where \(\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\) denotes the symmetric Algebra of \(\mathscr{F}\)).

(ii) the set of morphisms of S-functors from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{A}(X)\otimes\mathscr{F})\).

To prove i), by (11.1 a) and b)), it suffices to show that if \(\mathscr{F}\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), then \(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})\) is flat over S. By (EGA III 1.4.15.5) it suffices to see that if \(\mathscr{F}\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), the same holds for \(\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\), which is a corollary of a result due to Daniel Lazard, according to which every flat module over a ring is a filtered inductive limit of free modules of finite type.

Let us now prove ii). Let \(\alpha\) be a morphism of S-functors from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\); to it corresponds a homomorphism \(\rho\) of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{E}\) to \(\mathscr{A}(X\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})) = \mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\) such that if \(\eta\) is an S-section of X, the homomorphism \((\mathscr{A}(\eta)\otimes\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})})\circ\rho : \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{O}_S\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F}) = \mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\) corresponds to \(\alpha(\eta)\). But since \(\alpha(\eta)\in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))(S)\), \(\mathscr{A}(\eta)\otimes \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})}\) factors through the canonical injective homomorphism \(\mathscr{F}\to\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\). We thus have, for each S-section \(\eta\) of X, a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{E} \ar[r]^-{\rho} \ar[dr]_-{\mathscr{A}(\eta)\otimes\mathrm{id}} & \mathscr{A}(X)\otimes\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F}) \ar[d] \\ & \mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F}) } \ , \] which shows that \(\rho\) factors through the canonical injective homomorphism \(\mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\), hence \(\rho\) can be regarded as a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules from \(\mathscr{E}\) to \(\mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F}\).

Conversely, let \[ \rho \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{A}(X)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{F}) \subset \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}}\Bigl(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{A}\bigl(X\underset{S}{\times}\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})\bigr)\Bigr) ; \] to it corresponds a morphism \(\alpha\) of S-functors from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\). For every S-scheme T and every T-section \(\eta\) of \(X\times_S T\), \(\alpha(\eta)\) corresponds to \(\mathscr{A}(\eta)\otimes\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\otimes\mathscr{O}_T}\), a homomorphism from \(\mathscr{E}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T\) to \(\mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T\), hence \(\alpha(\eta)\in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))(T)\), which shows that \(\alpha\) is a morphism from X to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\), which proves ii).

Let G be an S-group, flat, quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, such that \(\mathscr{A} = \mathscr{A}(G)\) is a flat \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module, and \(\mathscr{E}\) a quasi-coherent and flat \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module. Note that a morphism of S-functors from G to \(\mathrm{End}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{W}(\mathscr{E})) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}),\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}))\) defines an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}\) if and only if the corresponding morphism \(\theta : G\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \to \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})\) satisfies the two following conditions:

1) if \(\mu : G\times_S G \to G\) denotes the structural morphism, the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ G\times_S G\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{id}_G\times\theta} \ar[d]_-{\mu\times\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})}} & G\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \ar[d]^-{\theta} \\ G\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \ar[r]_-{\theta} & \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) } \]
is commutative.

2) if \(\varepsilon : S \to G\) is the unit section of G, the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \ar[rr]^-{\varepsilon\times\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})}} \ar[dr]_-{\cong} & & G\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \ar[dl]^-{\theta} \\ & \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) & } \]
is commutative.
It then amounts to the same thing to say that the morphism \(\rho : \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{E}\) deduced from \(\theta\) satisfies the two following axioms:

(CM 1) If \(\Delta : \mathscr{A} \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{A} = \mathscr{A}(G\times_S G)\) is the homomorphism \(\mathscr{A}(\theta)\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E} & \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E} \ar[l]_-{\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{A}}\otimes\rho} \\ \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E} \ar[u]^-{\Delta\otimes\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{E}}} & \mathscr{E} \ar[l]^-{\rho} \ar[u]_-{\rho} } \]
is commutative.

(CM 2) If \(\eta : \mathscr{A} \to \mathscr{O}_S\) is the homomorphism \(\mathscr{A}(\varepsilon)\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{O}_S\otimes\mathscr{E} & \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E} \ar[l]_-{\eta\otimes\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{E}}} \\ & \mathscr{E} \ar[ul]^-{\cong} \ar[u]_-{\rho} } \]
is commutative.

It amounts to the same thing to say that the morphism \(\theta_{\mathrm{af}} : G_{\mathrm{af}}\times_S \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E}) \to \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{E})\) corresponding to \(\rho\) defines an action of \(G_{\mathrm{af}}\) on \(\mathscr{E}\) (compare I, 4.2).
Consequently, there is a one-to-one correspondence between actions of G on \(\mathscr{E}\) and actions of \(G_{\mathrm{af}}\) on \(\mathscr{E}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11.7

Let G be an S-group quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S, flat and such that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is a flat \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\mathscr{A}\), and \(\mathscr{E}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module. We suppose either that G is affine over S, or that \(\mathscr{E}\) is flat over S. Let \(\rho : \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}\) be a homomorphism defining an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}\). Let \(\mathscr{E}_0\) be a quasi-coherent sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module of \(\mathscr{E}\) such that the homomorphism \(\mathscr{E}_0 \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}\) deduced from \(\rho\) factors through the canonical homomorphism (injective, thanks to the fact that \(\mathscr{A}\) is flat!) \(\mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}_0 \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}\) by means of the homomorphism \(\rho_0 : \mathscr{E}_0 \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}_0\). Then \(\rho\) defines an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}_0\) (which will be called the induced action on \(\mathscr{E}_0\) by \(\rho\), and one says that \(\mathscr{E}_0\) is stable under \(\rho\)).
