This follows immediately from the definitions and from (11.6). One will note, however, that in general the canonical map \(\mathbb{W}(\mathscr{E}_0) \to \mathbb{W}(\mathscr{E})\) is not injective.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11.8

Let G be an S-group quasi-separated and quasi-compact over S such that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is a free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\mathscr{A}\) with basis \((\varphi_i)\), and \(\mathscr{E}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module. We suppose either that G is affine over S, or that G and \(\mathscr{E}\) are flat over S. Let \(\rho : \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}\) be a homomorphism defining an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(x\in \Gamma(S,\mathscr{E})\). Set \(\rho(x) = \sum \varphi_i\otimes x_i\), where the \(x_i\in\Gamma(S,\mathscr{E})\) are well determined by this relation. Then the submodule \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) generated by the \(x_i\) is stable under \(\rho\), quasi-coherent and of finite type over \(\mathscr{O}_S\), and \(x\in \Gamma(S,\mathscr{F})\).

It is clear that \(\mathscr{F}\) is quasi-coherent and of finite type. Axiom (CM 1) shows that \(\sum\Delta(\varphi_i)\otimes x_i = \sum\varphi_i\otimes\rho(x_i)\); on the other hand, \(\Delta(\varphi_i) = \sum\varphi_j\otimes a_{ij}\), whence \(\sum\varphi_j\otimes a_{ij}x_i = \sum\varphi_i\otimes\rho(x_i)\), whence, since the \(\varphi_i\) form a basis of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\rho(x_i) = \sum a_{ji}\otimes x_i\), where \(a_{ji}\in\Gamma(S,\mathscr{A})\), which shows that \(\rho(\mathscr{F})\subset \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{F}\), hence \(\mathscr{F}\) is stable under \(\rho\). Finally, axiom (CM 2) shows that \(x = \sum_i \eta(\varphi_i)x_i \in \Gamma(S,\mathscr{F})\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11.9

Let G be an S-group quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S such that \(\mathscr{A} = \mathscr{A}(G)\) becomes locally free after a faithfully flat quasi-compact extension of the base S (which is the case, for example, when G is a reductive group, as we shall see in XXV), and let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module. We suppose either that G is affine over S, or that G and \(\mathscr{E}\) are flat over S. Let \(\rho : \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{E}\) be a homomorphism defining an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}\). Then, for every quasi-coherent sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\mathscr{F}\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), there exists a smallest quasi-coherent sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) containing \(\mathscr{F}\) that is stable under \(\rho\). Its formation commutes with every base extension \(S' \to S\). When \(\mathscr{F}\) is of finite type, so is \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\).
One easily reduces, by faithfully flat descent, to the case where \(\mathscr{A}\) is a free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module, in which case the proposition is a consequence of lemma 11.8.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11.10

Let S be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, G an S-group quasi-compact and quasi-separated over S such that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) becomes locally free after a faithfully flat quasi-compact extension of the base S, and let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module. We suppose either that G is affine over S, or that G and \(\mathscr{E}\) are flat over S. Suppose given an action of G on \(\mathscr{E}\). Then \(\mathscr{E}\) is the inductive limit of an increasing filtered family of quasi-coherent sub-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules of finite type of \(\mathscr{E}\) stable under G.
This is an immediate consequence of (11.9), for \(\mathscr{E}\) is the inductive limit of its quasi-coherent sub-Modules of finite type (EGA I 9.4.9, IV 1.7.7).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 11.10.1

J.-P. Serre has remarked that in 11.9 (hence also in 11.10) it suffices to assume that for every affine open subset U of S, there exists a faithfully flat morphism \(U'\to U\), with U' affine with ring A', such that \(\mathscr{E}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_U}\mathscr{O}_{U'}\) is defined by a projective A'-module (not necessarily free). Moreover, when S is locally noetherian, regular and of dimension 1, this hypothesis can be replaced by the single hypothesis that G be flat over S (which implies that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is flat); on this subject see J.-P. Serre, Groupes de Grothendieck des schémas en groupes réductifs déployés, Pub. Math. IHES no. 39, p. 37-52.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11.11

Let G be an algebraic group over the field k. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    G is affine.

  2. (ii)

    G is quasi-affine.

  3. (iii)

    One can make G act faithfully on a quasi-affine k-scheme.

  4. (iv)

    One can make G act faithfully on a vector space over k (not necessarily of finite dimension).

  5. (v)

    G is isomorphic to a closed subgroup of a group \(\mathrm{GL}(n)_k\).

Proof. We have (i)⇒(ii) trivially, and (ii)⇒(iii), for G acts faithfully on itself by left translations. We have (iii)⇒(iv), for if G acts on X quasi-compact and quasi-separated over k, it also acts on the vector space \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\), thanks to the fact that the formation of \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) (\(f : X \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k\) the structural morphism) commutes with every base change \(S\to \mathrm{Spec}\,k\) (EGA IV 1.7.12); hence it acts on the affine envelope \(X' = \mathrm{Spec}(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X))\), and the canonical morphism \(X\to X'\) is evidently compatible with the action of G; this said, if X is quasi-affine, i.e. \(X\to X'\) is an open immersion, and if G acts faithfully on X, it acts faithfully on X', or what amounts to the same thing, on the vector space \(V = \Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\), which proves the implication (iii)⇒(iv).

Suppose now (iv), i.e. that G acts faithfully on the vector space V. Then, by virtue of 11.10, V is the inductive limit of finite-dimensional vector subspaces \(V_i\), stable under the action of G. If \(K_i\) is the kernel of the induced action of G on \(V_i\), i.e. of \(G\to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(V_i)\), then \(K_i\) is a closed subscheme of G, and the hypothesis that G acts faithfully is expressed at once by the fact that the intersection of the \(K_i\) is the unit subgroup of G. As G is noetherian, it follows that one of the \(K_i\) is already reduced to the unit group, hence that \(G\to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{V_i}\) is a monomorphism. It is therefore a closed immersion by virtue of 1.4.2, which proves that (iv)⇒(v). As (v)⇒(i) trivially, this proves 11.11.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 11.11.1

M. Raynaud has pointed out to us that one can generalize 11.11 to the case of a quasi-compact and quasi-separated group scheme G over a scheme S locally noetherian, regular, of dimension \(\leqslant 1\), assuming moreover G flat over S. One then has the equivalence of the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    G is affine over S.

  2. (ii)

    G is quasi-affine over S.

  3. (iii)

    One can make G act faithfully on a quasi-affine S-scheme.

  4. (iv)

    One can make G act faithfully on a quasi-coherent Module flat over S.

  5. (v)

    (If G is of finite type over S and S is noetherian), G is isomorphic to a closed subgroup of an \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(V)\), where V is a locally free Module of finite type over S.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11.12

Let k be a field, G a quasi-compact k-group. Set \(A = \mathscr{A}(G)\). Given \(x\in A\), there exists a finite type sub-k-algebra V of A such that \(x\in V\), \(\Delta(V)\subset V\otimes_k V\) and \(u(V)\subset V\), where u denotes the involution of A corresponding to the inverse map of G.
By 11.3, one can replace G by \(G_{\mathrm{af}}\), hence we may assume G affine.

Let \((\varphi_i)\) be a basis of A. Then \(\Delta(x) = \sum\varphi_i\otimes a_i\) and \(\Delta(\varphi_i) = \sum\varphi_j\otimes b_{ji}\); axiom (HA 1) of (I, 2) shows that \(\sum\varphi_i\otimes\Delta(a_i) = \sum\Delta(\varphi_i)\otimes a_i\), whence \(\Delta(a_i) = \sum b_{ij}\otimes a_j\). Let then V be the sub-k-algebra of A generated by the \(b_{ij}\) and the \(u(b_{ij})\). It is clear that V is a k-algebra of finite type. Axiom (HA 1) also shows that \(\sum\Delta(\varphi_j)\otimes b_{ji} = \sum\varphi_j\otimes \Delta(b_{ji})\), and one deduces that \(\Delta(b_{ij}) = \sum b_{ik}\otimes b_{kj}\) and that \(\Delta(u(b_{ij})) = \sum u(b_{kj})\otimes u(b_{ik})\). Since \(\Delta\) is an algebra homomorphism, one deduces that \(\Delta(V)\subset V\otimes_k V\) and \(u(V)\subset V\). Finally, axiom (HA 2) of (I, 2) shows that \(a_i = \sum\eta(a_j)b_{ij}\) and that \(x = \sum\eta(\varphi_i)a_i\), so that \(x\in V\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11.13

Let k be a field and G an affine k-group with algebra A. Then G is the projective limit of an increasing filtered system of affine k-groups of finite type, whose transition morphisms are faithfully flat.

As the set of finite type sub-k-algebras of A stable under \(\Delta\) and u is stable under generation, A is the inductive limit of an increasing filtered family \((B_i)_{i\in I}\) of finite type sub-k-algebras of A stable under \(\Delta\) and u. Then each algebra \(B_i\), equipped with the homomorphism \(B_i \to B_i\otimes_k B_i\) deduced from \(\Delta\) and with the restriction of u to \(B_i\), is equipped with a structure of involutive augmented associative hyperalgebra, hence (I, 2) is the k-algebra of an affine k-group \(G_i\), of finite type over k. Finally, since \(A = \varinjlim V_i\), \(G = \varprojlim G_i\) (EGA IV 8.2.3). The transition morphisms are faithfully flat by the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11.14

Let k be a field, G and H two affine k-groups of finite type, \(u : G \to H\) a morphism of k-groups, and \(u^{\natural} : B \to A\) the corresponding homomorphism of k-algebras. For u to be faithfully flat, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u^{\natural}\) be injective.

The condition is evidently necessary (EGA IV 2.2.3 and \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 6.6.1). Let us show that it is sufficient. Set \(K = \mathrm{Ker}\,u\). Then G/K is a k-group of finite type (VIA 2), and u factors as \(G \xrightarrow{\ p\ } G/K \xrightarrow{\ v\ } H\), where p is faithfully flat (VIA 2) and v is a monomorphism of k-groups of finite type, hence a closed immersion (1.4.2). Since H is an affine scheme, and v is a closed immersion, G/K is an affine scheme (EGA I 4.2.3), and, if we denote by C the algebra of G/K, the homomorphism \(v^{\natural} : B \to C\) is surjective (loc. cit.). Now, since \(u^{\natural}\) is injective, and \(u^{\natural} = p^{\natural}\circ v^{\natural}\), \(v^{\natural}\) is also injective: it is an isomorphism, hence v is an isomorphism, and, since p is faithfully flat, the same holds for u.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 11.15

Given a field k, a k-vector space V, a k-group G acting on V, and a vector \(v\in V\), we say that v is semi-invariant under G if the vector subspace \(kv\) is stable under G.

Denote by A the algebra of G, and by \(\rho : V \to A\otimes_k V\) the linear map defining the action of G on V. Saying that v is semi-invariant under G amounts to saying that there exists \(\lambda\in A\) such that, for every \(w\in kv\), \(\rho(w) = \lambda\otimes w\). When \(v\neq 0\), the element \(\lambda\in A\) is well determined by this relation; it will be called the weight of the semi-invariant element \(v\).

Saying that v is semi-invariant of invariant \(\lambda\) amounts to saying that \(\varphi(g,v) = \lambda(g)v\) for every \(g\in G(k)\), where \(\lambda : G \to \mathbb{G}_{m,k}\) is the morphism defined by \(\lambda\). One sees therefore that \(\lambda\) is a character of G, called the character associated to the semi-invariant element v.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 11.16 (Chevalley)

Let k be a field, G an affine algebraic k-group, H a closed group subscheme of G. Then there exists a finite number of linearly independent elements \(a_i\) of the algebra A of G, such that H is the largest closed group subscheme of G under which the \(a_i\) are semi-invariant; one may moreover assume that all the \(a_i\) have the same weight under H.

Recall that, since G is affine, so is H (I 4.2.3). Let A (resp. B) be the algebra of G (resp. H); by EGA I 4.2.3, there exists an ideal I of A such that B is isomorphic to A/I. Since G acts on itself by left translations, it also acts on A, this action being expressed by the diagonal map \(\Delta : A \to A\otimes_k A\) (I, 2 and 7.2). Since G is of finite type over k, A is of finite type over k, hence I admits a finite system of generators \((g_1,\ldots,g_r)\). It follows from (11.9) that there exists a finite-dimensional vector subspace V of A stable under G and containing each of the \(g_i\). Then set \(W = V\cap I\); it is a vector space over k of finite dimension, whose dimension we shall denote by d. Since V contains all the \(g_i\), W generates the ideal I. Denote by \(p : A \to A/I = B\) the canonical map, and by \(q = p\otimes\mathrm{id}_A : A\otimes_k A \to (A/I)\otimes_k A\). Then the action of H on A is determined by \(q\circ\Delta : A \to B\otimes_k A\). Since H is a group subscheme of G, the diagonal map \(A/I \to (A/I)\otimes_k(A/I) = ((A/I)\otimes_k A)/((A/I)\otimes_k I)\) is deduced from \(q\circ\Delta\) by passage to the quotient, which shows that \((q\circ\Delta)(I) \subset (A/I)\otimes_k I\); in other words, I is stable under H. Since V is stable under G, hence under H, we see that W is stable under H.

Set \(E = \bigwedge^d V\), let \((w_1,\ldots,w_d)\) be a basis of W over k, and let \((e_0,\ldots,e_n)\) be a basis of E over k containing \(e_0 = w_1\wedge\cdots\wedge w_d\). The action of G on V determines canonically an action of G on \(\bigwedge^d V\), which defines a linear map \(\rho : E \to A\otimes_k E\). Set \(\rho(e_i) = \sum a^j_i\otimes e_j\), and \(a_i = a^0_i\). Since W is stable under H, if we set \(\sigma = (p\otimes\mathrm{id}_E)\circ\rho : E \to (A/I)\otimes_k E\), it is clear that \(\sigma(e_0)\) is proportional to \(e_0\); in other words, for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\), we have \(a_i \in I\). Axiom (CM 1) of (I, 7.2) applied to \(\rho(e_0) = \sum a_i\otimes e_i\) shows that \(\sum\Delta(a_i)\otimes e_i = \sum a_i\otimes\rho(e_i) = \sum a_i\otimes a^j_i\otimes e_j\), from which one deduces that \(\Delta(a_i) = a_0\otimes a_i + \sum_{j\geqslant 1} a_j\otimes a^i_j\); hence, since for \(j\geqslant 1\), \(a_j\in I\), we see that \(q\circ\Delta(a_i) = p(a_0)\otimes a_i\), so that for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\), \(a_i\) is semi-invariant under H with weight \(p(a_0)\), independent of i. Extract from the system \((a_1,\ldots,a_n)\) a maximal system of linearly independent vectors, which we may assume is written \((a_1,\ldots,a_m)\). Then the \(a_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), are linearly independent, semi-invariant under H, and of the same weight.

Conversely, let H' be a closed group subscheme of G under which each of the \(a_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\)) is semi-invariant. Let us show that \(H^1 = H\). There exists likewise an ideal I' of A such that the algebra B' of H' is isomorphic to A/I'; denote by \(p' : A \to A/I'\) the canonical map, and by \(q' = p'\otimes\mathrm{id}_A\). By hypothesis, for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\), there exists \(\lambda_i \in B'\) such that \(q'\circ\Delta(a_i) = \sum p'(a_j)\otimes a^i_j = \lambda_i\otimes a_i\). Now axiom (CM 2) of (I, 7.2) shows that \(e_i = \sum\eta(a^i_j)e_j\), hence that \(\eta(a^i_j) = \delta_{ij}\) (Kronecker symbol); the equality \(\sum p'(a_j)\otimes\eta(a^i_j) = \lambda_i\otimes\eta(a_i)\) thus reads \(p'(a_i) = 0\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\), for \(\eta(a_i) = \eta(a^0_i) = 0\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\); consequently, we have \(a_i \in I'\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant m\), so that \(a_i \in I'\) for \(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\). Hence, if we set \(\sigma' = (p'\otimes \mathrm{id}_E)\circ\Delta\), \(\sigma'(e_0) = \sum p'(a_i)\otimes e_i\) is proportional to \(e_0\), so that \(q'\circ\Delta(W) \subset (A/I')\otimes_k W\); in other words, W is stable under H'. Since W generates the ideal I, I is stable under H', hence \(q'\circ\Delta(I) \subset (A/I')\otimes_k I\), and \(q'\circ\Delta\) defines by passage to the quotient a linear map \(A/I \to (A/I')\otimes_k(A/I) = ((A/I')\otimes_k A)/((A/I')\otimes_k I)\), hence the restriction to \(H'\times_k H\) of the multiplication morphism of G factors through H, which implies that H' is a group subscheme of H, q.e.d.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 11.17 (Chevalley)

Let k be a field, G an affine k-group (not necessarily algebraic), and H a closed group subscheme of G normal in G; then the quotient sheaf (for the (fpqc) topology) G/H is representable by an affine k-group.

First case: Suppose first G algebraic. By (VIA 2), the cokernel K of the injection \(H \to G\) exists, the canonical morphism \(p : G \to K\) is covering for the (fpqc) topology, and K represents the quotient sheaf G/H for the (fpqc) topology; it therefore remains to show that K is affine.

Suppose first k algebraically closed, G reduced and connected and H reduced. By (BIBLE 4, cor. of th. 1), there exists a finite-dimensional linear representation \(\rho : G \to \mathrm{GL}(V)\) such that \((\mathrm{Ker}\,\rho)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} = H\). By 11.11, \(G/\mathrm{Ker}\,\rho\) is affine, hence \((G/H)/(\mathrm{Ker}\,\rho/H)\) is affine, hence also \(G/H\), the group \(\mathrm{Ker}\,\rho/H\) being finite.
If k is algebraically closed, and G and H reduced, then \((G/H)/(G^0/H\cap G^0)\) is finite, as a quotient of \(G/G^0\), and \(G^0/H\cap G^0\) is affine, hence G/H is affine.

In the case where k is algebraically closed, and G and H arbitrary, \((G\times_k H)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is isomorphic to \((G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})\times_k(H_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})\), hence \((G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})/(H_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})\) is isomorphic to \((G/H)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\). Since \((G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})/(H_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}})\) is affine, so is G/H (EGA I 5.1.10), for, since G/H is of finite type over k, its nilradical is nilpotent.

In the case where k is arbitrary, \((G\otimes_k \overline{k})/(H\otimes_k \overline{k})\) is isomorphic to \((G/H)\otimes_k \overline{k}\) (9.2 v)); hence, since the first is affine, so is the second, hence G/H is affine.

Second case: General case. By (11.13), the algebra A of G is the inductive limit of an increasing filtered family \((A_i)_{i\in I}\) of finite type subalgebras of A stable under the diagonal map and the involution. By (EGA I 4.2.3), H is affine, and if we denote by B the algebra of H, the homomorphism \(A\to B\) deduced from the injection \(H\to G\) is surjective. Set \(I = \mathrm{Ker}(A\to B)\), \(I_i = I\cap A_i\) and \(B_i = A_i/I_i\); since the structure of involutive augmented associative hyperalgebra of A passes to the quotient through I, one easily checks that that of \(A_i\) passes to the quotient through \(I_i\), so that \(B_i\) is the algebra of an affine k-group of finite type \(H_i\); it is clear that \(B = \varinjlim B_i\). By (EGA I 4.2.3), \(H_i\) is isomorphic to a closed group subscheme of \(G_i\).

By (6.7), the fact that H is normal in G is expressed by saying that a certain morphism \(H\times_k G \to G\) factors through the injection \(H\to G\); in other words, that the corresponding homomorphism \(A \to B\otimes_k A\) factors through the canonical homomorphism \(A \to B\); one easily checks that the homomorphism \(A_i \to B_i\otimes_k A_i\) defined in an analogous way factors through \(A_i \to B_i\), hence that \(H_i\) is normal in \(G_i\). By what was seen above, \(G_i\) being of finite type over k, \(G_i/H_i\) is representable by an affine k-group \(K_i\), whose algebra we shall denote by \(C_i\). Then set \(C = \varinjlim C_i\), and let \(K = \varprojlim K_i\) be the affine k-scheme with algebra C (EGA IV 8.2.3).

Let us show that K represents G/H; for this, it suffices to check that \(H\times_k G \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } G\times_k G\), and that the morphism \(G\to K\) is covering for the (fpqc) topology (IV, 4.3). Now for each i, we have \(H_i\times_k G_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } G_i\times_{k_i} G_i\), hence the same holds for the morphism obtained by passing to the projective limit; finally, each of the morphisms \(G_i \to K_i\) is faithfully flat (9.2 (xi)), in other words \(C_i\) is faithfully flat over \(A_i\); since \(A = \varinjlim A_i\) and \(C = \varinjlim C_i\), one deduces that C is faithfully flat over A, so that \(G\to K\) is a faithfully flat morphism. Since this morphism is affine, it is quasi-compact, hence covering for the (fpqc) topology, q.e.d.
